comment: Audit Sweep A (lenses N1-N3, N5-N8) — adversarially verified. C1/C2 fixed in 1e5cab3 before this record was written.

confirmed.title: Restructure realMove/convert DB sync is batch-end, busy-timeout-less, and error-swallowed — WAL contention with the engine writer (or a mid-batch crash) strands the entire moved batch, and the next scan's orphan sweep permanently destroys tags, captions, and manual person assignments for every moved file (sibling call site missed by the M5/L5 rename fix)
confirmed.line: ReadStore.swift:887-903 (updatePathTexts); RestructureView.swift:1393-1394, 1412-1414 (apply realMove), 1448-1450, 1455-1457 (convertSymlinksToMoves); FileIDEngineMain.swift:625-664 (orphan sweep purge)
confirmed.evidence: updatePathTexts opens `let q = try DatabaseQueue(path: dbURL.path)` (ReadStore.swift:890) with NO Configuration — GRDB's default busyMode is .immediateError — and on any error only sets `self.lastError = "Path update failed: \(error)"` (900-902), which RestructureView never displays (grep: zero `lastError` references in RestructureView.swift). The same file's rename path was explicitly fixed for this exact class: renameWriteQueue (ReadStore.swift:1037-1041) sets `config.busyMode = .timeout(5)` with the comment "momentary WAL contention with the engine writer retries instead of failing immediately (which used to strand the file: renamed on disk but the DB row still pointing at oldPath)" — R0's verifiedClean even pins "M5/L5 applyProposedName busy-timeout + disk rollback ... intact". But the restructure call sites never got the fix: RestructureEngine.apply moves files one-by-one (`try fm.moveItem(at: oldURL, to: newURL); pathUpdates.append((p.fileID, newURL.path))`, RestructureView.swift:1393-1394) and only AFTER the whole loop calls `await store.updatePathTexts(pathUpdates)` (1412-1414); convertSymlinksToMoves does the same (1455-1457). There is no per-move DB write, no rollback of the on-disk moves on DB failure, and no recovery sidecar (the Windows apply has one). Downstream: macOS DBWriter computes no content_hash/file_ref (per R0 verdict: "macOS never populates content_hash"), so a re-scan inserts moved files as brand-new rows, and the orphan sweep deletes the old rows — `fm.fileExists(atPath: row.path) ? nil : row.id` (FileIDEngineMain.swift:628) then `DELETE FROM files WHERE id IN (...)` with face_prints cascade (656-659).
confirmed.trigger: User runs Deep Analyze or a scan (the engine writer commits batches continuously — RestructureView itself prompts users to run Deep Analyze before restructuring, and nothing in applySelected/convertAllToRealMoves (RestructureView.swift:827-852) guards against an active engine job). While it runs, the user clicks "Convert to real moves" on a 3,000-file plan. Every file is moved on disk; the single batch-end `q.write` then hits the engine's in-flight write transaction, GRDB's .immediateError busyMode throws SQLITE_BUSY, the catch swallows it into the never-shown lastError, and the UI reports "Converted 3000 shortcuts to real moves". All 3,000 rows still carry the old path_text. Same end state if the app crashes or is force-quit mid-batch (cross-volume moves can take minutes; zero DB writes have happened yet). Next scan of the root: old paths are gone → orphan sweep cascade-deletes all 3,000 rows (including face_prints person assignments and user tags); the files re-enter as fresh rows with no heal (no content_hash on macOS).
confirmed.impact: Silent, permanent loss of derived and user-entered data for the entire restructure batch: manual person assignments, person-cluster associations, user tags, VLM captions/smart names all destroyed; Library/preview shows dead entries until the scan, then ghost-free but amnesiac rows. The confirmation dialog already warns "This isn't reversible inside the app" — but the user did nothing wrong; the moves succeeded and the bookkeeping was dropped without any error surfaced.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Three-part: (1) give updatePathTexts the same `config.busyMode = .timeout(5)` the M5 fix gave renameWriteQueue; (2) move the DB update into the per-move loop (move file → update row → on DB failure, move the file back, mirroring applyProposedName's rollback at ReadStore.swift:1074-1078) so a crash strands at most one file; (3) surface a partial-failure status ("N moved but M rows not updated — rescan recommended") instead of swallowing into lastError. Optionally mirror the Windows recovery-sidecar pattern for crash resilience.

verdict.reasoning:
Set out to refute and could not — every load-bearing claim checks out at HEAD (5e5289c), and the finding is not recorded in any of the three audit records.

Mechanics verified independently:
1. The unconfigured connection is real: /Users/adamnolle/Desktop/Code/FileID/platforms/apple/app/Sources/FileID/Database/ReadStore.swift:890 `let q = try DatabaseQueue(path: dbURL.path)` with no Configuration. GRDB's default is pinned in the vendored source: .build/checkouts/GRDB.swift/GRDB/Core/Configuration.swift:331 `public var busyMode: Database.BusyMode = .immediateError`. The entire batch is ONE write transaction (ReadStore.swift:891-898), so a single SQLITE_BUSY loses all N row updates atomically.
2. The error is genuinely invisible: the catch at ReadStore.swift:900-902 only sets `self.lastError`. Grep across the whole app: zero readers of ReadStore.lastError — every `.lastError` UI reference (Sidebar.swift:65/107, WelcomeSheet.swift:116/119) is EngineClient.lastError, a different property. ApplyResult carries only disk-move counts, so RestructureView.swift:837/849 reports "Converted N shortcuts to real moves · skipped 0 · failed 0" even when zero DB rows updated.
3. The sibling-fix asymmetry is exact: renameWriteQueue (ReadStore.swift:1037-1041) sets `config.busyMode = .timeout(5)` with the cited strand-the-file comment; applyProposedName has per-file DB-failure disk rollback (1074-1078) and undoRenames has DB-agree rollback (1000-1014). R0-findings.json:251 pins M5/L5 as covering "the only rename/undo paths" — updatePathTexts was never in scope.
4. Call sites match: RestructureEngine.apply moves files one-by-one (RestructureView.swift:1393-1394) collecting pathUpdates, then one batch-end `await store.updatePathTexts(pathUpdates)` (1412-1414); convertSymlinksToMoves identical (1448-1450, 1455-1457). No per-move DB write, no disk rollback on DB failure, no sidecar. The Windows engine DOES have both (restructure_apply.rs:12 "failed update is also recorded to a recovery sidecar", :173-180 per-move handling) — the parity-gap claim is accurate.
5. No guard against a concurrent engine writer: applySelected (827-841) and convertAllToRealMoves (843-852) launch unconditionally. The Restructure tab itself hosts a "Run Deep Analyze" button (RestructureView.swift:285) and recomputes proposals on Deep Analyze terminal events (227-236), so engine-writes-during-restructure is a designed flow, not an exotic race. The engine writer commits multi-file batch transactions every ~200ms during scans (DBWriter.swift:100-180 — batches deliberately grown to ~30 files to amortize commits), each holding the WAL write lock; an .immediateError single-shot write colliding with one throws immediately.
6. The crash/force-quit trigger stands with no contention needed: zero DB writes happen until the whole move loop completes, so killing the app mid-batch strands every already-moved file.
7. The destruction step is real and unhealed: sweepOrphans runs post-scan (FileIDEngineMain.swift:561), selects rows under the scan root with scanned_at < scanStart, stats them (628 `fm.fileExists(atPath: row.path) ? nil : row.id`), and DELETEs in chunks (654-659) with ON DELETE CASCADE on tags/ocr_text/face_prints/clip_embeddings plus reconcilePersons; cap 5000 covers a 3000-file batch. The macOS scan path binds content_hash/file_ref as NULL (DBWriter.swift:373 comment + :417-418), the upsert is keyed on path_text (382), and grep finds no heal logic anywhere in the apple engine — moved files re-enter as fresh amnesiac rows. The default destination root is the recent scan-session root (RestructureView.swift:219-222), so moved files land back under the swept root. vlm_description/vlm_proposed_name live on the deleted row; person assignments live in cascade-deleted face_prints.
8. Dialog text confirmed at RestructureView.swift:193. No test pins updatePathTexts (zero hits in platforms/apple/Tests).

Duplicate check: grep for updatePathTexts across shared/docs/audit-2026-06-09-merge/R0-findings.json, shared/docs/audit-2026-06-04c/*, and shared/docs/AUDIT-2026-06-03.md returns zero hits; the restructure entries there are Windows-side (long-path moves, Keep-tier selection, B4 stale gate) and R0's orphan-sweep mentions are verifiedClean composition notes. Not a duplicate.

Caveats (do not change verdict): the WAL-contention trigger is probabilistic per batch write (one instant of collision), not guaranteed every run — but the crash-mid-batch trigger is deterministic given a mid-batch termination, any other write error (disk full, locked) reaches the same swallowed path, and the silent-success UX means no user-visible signal exists in any failure mode. Severity stays high: silent, permanent, unrecoverable loss of user-entered data (manual person assignments, tags) plus all derived data for an entire restructure batch, behind a success message, in a first-class feature whose own dialog stresses irreversibility. Not critical because it needs the realMove/convert flow plus a failure coincidence, and the app is pre-release with no existing-user fleet. The three-part suggested fix matches the verified code structure (timeout config exists at 1037-1041 to copy; per-move rollback pattern exists at 1074-1078 to mirror).
verdict.experiment: Not needed — confirmed by static trace. If hardware verification is desired: on a Mac, start a scan of a large corpus, and while DBWriter batches are committing, apply a realMove restructure; observe "moved" status with stale path_text rows (SELECT path_text FROM files WHERE id IN ...), then rescan and observe the rows (and their tags/face_prints) deleted by orphan_sweep.

confirmed.title: macOS restructure apply has no SEC-5/SEC-7 equivalent: no apply-time root containment and no symlink-ancestor check — createDirectory/moveItem through a symlinked bucket directory silently relocates files outside the user's chosen root (apply() doesn't even receive the root)
confirmed.evidence: The live apply path is `static func apply(proposals: [RestructureView.Proposal], store: ReadStore, mode: ApplyMode)` (RestructureView.swift:1366-1367) — note the signature has no libraryRoot parameter, so no containment re-check is even possible. It runs `try fm.createDirectory(at: newURL.deletingLastPathComponent(), withIntermediateDirectories: true)` (1379-1380) then `try fm.moveItem(at: oldURL, to: newURL)` (1393) with zero symlink awareness — grep over app/engine/shared Sources finds exactly one symlink check in all destructive flows (the typeSymbolicLink guard at 1434, in a different function). The only containment check is at plan time and is lexical: `let resolvedTarget = target.standardizedFileURL.path ... guard resolvedTarget.hasPrefix(resolvedRoot + "/")` (1221-1223) — standardizedFileURL removes "."/".." components but does NOT resolve symlinks. Contrast Windows: DECISIONS.md 2026-05-17 "SEC-5 TOCTOU pre+post check on restructure apply (V15.8)" added has_reparse_point_in_chain BEFORE and AFTER create_dir_all, plus SEC-7 root containment, specifically because "An attacker holding a handle to a pre-existing directory under library_root could plant a junction BEFORE create_dir_all and silently redirect the move outside the root." No macOS audit record covers this (all SEC-5/SEC-7 and restructure_apply findings cite .rs files).
confirmed.trigger: The user's chosen root contains a symlinked directory whose name collides with a generated bucket component — e.g. `root/Photos` → /Volumes/External/Photos (a common media-library layout), `root/Documents` → iCloud/another volume, or a symlink delivered inside an extracted ZIP/tar (archives preserve symlinks) — or a local process swaps one in between plan and apply (the plan-time lexical check passes either way). User clicks "Convert to real moves": createDirectory follows the link and creates Photos/2023/... at the link's real target, then moveItem relocates every matching file there. DB path_text records the link-routed path (1394).
confirmed.impact: Files are physically moved outside the folder the user chose — violating the containment promise the Windows port ships hardening for — into a location they may not control or back up. If the symlink is later removed or retargeted, every recorded path dangles: the orphan sweep purges the rows (tags/captions/person assignments lost) while the files sit at the forgotten real target; the user sees their library shrink with no explanation. Symlink mode (1391) likewise plants the new tree outside root.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Pass libraryRoot into apply()/convertSymlinksToMoves. Before each move: (a) verify `newURL.deletingLastPathComponent().resolvingSymlinksInPath().path` is prefix-contained in `root.resolvingSymlinksInPath().path` (resolved-to-resolved comparison so a symlinked root itself stays valid); (b) after createDirectory, re-verify (pre+post, mirroring SEC-5's V15.8 pattern) by lstat-walking ancestors from root down (URLResourceKey.isSymbolicLinkKey) and skipping the move with a reported conflict on any symlink component. Also note the open parity question already seeded for Windows: macOS reports collisions as skip+conflict while Windows uniquifies with " (2)" — resolve in the parity phase, do not silently change here.

verdict.reasoning:
Set out to refute and could not — every load-bearing claim survives independent trace at HEAD of fix/bug-audit-sweep.

MECHANICS VERIFIED: /Users/adamnolle/Desktop/Code/FileID/platforms/apple/app/Sources/FileID/Views/RestructureView.swift — the live apply is `static func apply(proposals:store:mode:)` (1366-1367, inside `enum RestructureEngine` at 1102) with NO libraryRoot parameter; `fm.createDirectory(at: newURL.deletingLastPathComponent(), withIntermediateDirectories: true)` (1379-1380) then `createSymbolicLink` (1391) / `moveItem` (1393) with zero symlink awareness; DB path_text records the link-routed `newURL.path` (1394). `convertSymlinksToMoves` (1421-1460) is equally root-blind. The only containment check is plan-time and lexical: `standardizedFileURL` prefix compare (1221-1223) removes "."/".." but does not resolve symlinks — and since the target is built by appending sanitized components to root, it passes trivially and provides no symlink protection.

LIVE-PATH CONFIRMED: UI apply buttons (RestructureView.swift:177) → applySelected (827-841) → RestructureEngine.apply (834); convertAllToRealMoves (843-852). This is the only reachable restructure apply on macOS.

STRONGEST CONFIRMATION (kills the finding's one inaccuracy but strengthens its substance): the macOS engine ALREADY ports SEC-5/SEC-7 — /Users/adamnolle/Desktop/Code/FileID/platforms/apple/engine/Sources/FileIDEngine/Pipeline/Restructure.swift:262-292 has an explicit "SEC-7 port" pre-check and "SEC-5 port" post-createDirectory re-check using resolvingSymlinksInPath() (containedInRoot, 327-332), commented "a symlinked bucket component must not let a move escape the tree the user authorized." But it is DEAD CODE: `.applyRestructure` IPC returns not_implemented_yet (FileIDEngineMain.swift:360-379, "handled by the FileID app on macOS") and grep finds zero callers of Restructure.apply. So the finding's evidence claim "exactly one symlink check in all destructive flows" is literally false — yet the live destructive flow indeed has none, and the project's own code defines this exact behavior as a containment violation. The hardening was applied to the unreachable engine path while the shipped app path was missed.

GUARD HUNT (all failed to kill it): (a) Sandbox — FileID.entitlements is an empty <dict/> (no com.apple.security.app-sandbox); EngineClient.swift:594 confirms "outside a sandbox it's just rootURL.path" — nothing blocks writes through a symlink to anywhere user-writable. (b) Test pins — platforms/apple/Tests has no restructure-apply or symlink containment tests. (c) Plan-time sanitization (sanitizePathSegment, 1465-1469) strips ".."/"/" from bucket names but cannot defend against an on-disk symlinked directory component. (d) The skip-identical and EEXIST handling (1377, 1397-1409) do not interfere with the trigger.

TRIGGER TRACE: root/Photos → /Volumes/External/Photos (user-made media layout, archive-extracted symlink, or attacker swap between plan and apply). Plan passes (lexical). Apply realMove: createDirectory traverses the symlinked "Photos" component and creates 2023/03-Mar at the external target; moveItem relocates each file there (cross-volume = copy+delete); DB records the lexical link-routed path. If the link is later removed/retargeted, paths dangle and sweepOrphans (FileIDEngineMain.swift:561/576) purges the rows on the next scan — tags/captions/person assignments lost while files sit at the forgotten real target. Symlink mode plants the link tree at the external target. DECISIONS.md:2609 (2026-05-17 SEC-5 V15.8 entry) matches the finding's quoted attacker rationale verbatim, confirming the cross-platform contrast.

DUPLICATE CHECK (all three named locations, negative): shared/docs/audit-2026-06-09-merge/R0-findings.json — all titles are Windows findings or unrelated macOS issues (CLIP rescan skip, IPC casing, etc.); no macOS restructure-containment entry. shared/docs/audit-2026-06-04c/ — Windows app/engine focused; its RestructureView.swift mentions are the Anchor/Keep divergence, a different bug. shared/docs/AUDIT-2026-06-03.md — restructure entries cite .rs/.cs only. Closest relative is AUDIT-2026-06-01.md PAR-72/73/75 (macOS engine restructure-apply lacked root-containment) — but that targeted engine Restructure.swift:262-294, where the fix subsequently landed in dead code; the live app-side path was never covered, and that doc is not in the dedup list anyway.

SEVERITY: keeping medium. Real containment violation with second-order metadata loss (orphan sweep) and explicit parity break with shipped Windows hardening (SEC-5/SEC-7) plus the project's own dead macOS port proving intent — but it requires a symlink in root colliding with a generated bucket name (Photos/Documents/People/Places/Misc — plausible, not default), the local-attacker model is weak for a desktop app, and files are relocated (initially still reachable via the link-routed path), not destroyed. SUGGESTED FIX is sound and cheap: pass libraryRoot into apply()/convertSymlinksToMoves and reuse the already-written containedInRoot pre+post pattern from engine Restructure.swift:262-292.
verdict.experiment: Not required (confirmed). For hardware UAT on the user's Mac: mkdir -p /tmp/fidroot /tmp/external; ln -s /tmp/external /tmp/fidroot/Photos; scan /tmp/fidroot with a dated photo; run Restructure → Convert to real moves; verify the file lands under /tmp/external/2023/... (physically outside /tmp/fidroot) and files.path_text records /tmp/fidroot/Photos/2023/... — then rm the symlink, rescan, and observe sweepOrphans purge the row.

confirmed.title: macOS scan-path PDF render has no pixel cap: fixed 2x scale of MediaBox allocates w*h*4 bytes per page (multi-GB for plotter/CAD/poster PDFs), x3 pages x up to 32 concurrent workers
confirmed.line: Tagging.swift:233-247 (gate at 207); concurrency: FileIDEngineMain.swift:488-489 + Hardware.swift:45-48; bounded contrast: DeepAnalyze.swift:694-698
confirmed.evidence: processPDF renders OCR pages at a FIXED scale with no pixel bound: `let bounds = page.getBoxRect(.mediaBox); let scale: CGFloat = 2.0; let w = Int(bounds.width * scale); let h = Int(bounds.height * scale); guard w > 0, h > 0 else { continue }` then `CGContext(data: nil, width: w, height: h, bitsPerComponent: 8, bytesPerRow: 0, ...)` and `ctx.fill(CGRect(x: 0, y: 0, width: w, height: h))` (Tagging.swift:235-247) — data:nil allocates w*h*4 bytes and fill commits every page. The only gate is FILE size: `if sizeMB > 20` (line 207) — vector PDFs with huge pages are tiny. The repo already has the bounded version of this exact code on the Deep Analyze path: `let scale = CGFloat(maxPixelSize) / longSide` (DeepAnalyze.swift:695), proving the scan path predates the bound. Up to 3 pages per file (line 231), and up to `Hardware.workerCap` files concurrently (P+E+P/2, capped 32 — Hardware.swift:45-48) via `pool.with { Tagging.processFile }` (FileIDEngineMain.swift:531-533). No prior audit record mentions processPDF/mediaBox (grep over AUDIT-2026-06-01/03, audit-2026-06-04b/c, R0-findings.json).
confirmed.trigger: Scan a folder containing large-format vector PDFs — CAD/plotter drawings, GIS maps, sewing/projector patterns, posters. An ARCH-E 36x48in page (2592x3456pt) renders a 5184x6912 context = 143 MB per page; the PDF-spec-max 14400x14400pt page renders 28800x28800 = 3.3 GB per page, fully committed by ctx.fill. Discovery sorts by path, so a folder of such PDFs hits many workers simultaneously: 14-32 concurrent renders x 100MB-3GB each.
confirmed.impact: Engine resident memory balloons by GBs mid-scan: system-wide memory pressure/swap storm (Mac beachballs), and in the extreme the engine is OOM-killed mid-scan — the app reports an engine crash and the killed file is never written with failed=1 (death happens before DBWriter), so every rescan re-hits the same PDFs and the scan of that library can never complete.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Bound the render like DeepAnalyze.renderFirstPDFPage: scale = min(2.0, maxPixels/longSide) with a hard pixel cap (e.g. 2048px long side for OCR, or 50 MP parity with Windows MAX_DECODED_PIXELS); skip pages whose mediaBox would exceed the cap and tag the file Large_Document.

verdict.reasoning: Independent trace confirms every load-bearing link. (1) The unbounded render exists exactly as cited: /Users/adamnolle/Desktop/Code/FileID/platforms/apple/engine/Sources/FileIDEngine/Pipeline/Tagging.swift:233-249 — fixed `scale = 2.0` of `getBoxRect(.mediaBox)`, `CGContext(data: nil, width: w, height: h, ...)` allocating w*h*4, `ctx.fill` committing the full buffer, up to 3 pages per file (line 231). The only gate is FILE size (`if sizeMB > 20`, line 207), which vector CAD/plotter/poster PDFs (small bytes, huge mediaBox) pass. (2) Concurrency multiplier survives all kill-attempts: visionQueue is `.concurrent` (Tagging.swift:23-27, no serialization); VisionWorkerPool(count: workerCap) admits exactly workerCap concurrent processFile calls (VisionWorker.swift:223-256); workerCap = P+E+max(1,P/2) capped 32 (Hardware.swift:45-48); FileIDEngineMain.swift:488-489 + 519-533 runs workerCap worker tasks through `pool.with { Tagging.processFile }`. No memory-pressure throttle exists anywhere — Hardware.residentMB/availableMemoryMB are only read for progress/log events (ScanCoordinator.swift:200-201, DBWriter.swift:264-297). (3) PDFs are routed to this path (Discovery.swift:47 → Tagging.swift:49-50) and discovery sorts by path (Discovery.swift:125), so a folder of large-format PDFs floods all workers contiguously. (4) The bounded contrast is real: DeepAnalyze.swift:687-710 renderFirstPDFPage uses `scale = maxPixelSize/longSide` on the same render code. (5) Math verified: ARCH-E 2592x3456pt x2 = 5184x6912 ≈ 143 MB/page; spec-max 14400x14400pt → 28800x28800 ≈ 3.3 GB/page, committed by fill. (6) Not a duplicate: grep for processPDF/mediaBox/getBoxRect/drawPDFPage/CGPDFDocument/pixel-cap over shared/docs/audit-2026-06-09-merge/R0-findings.json, shared/docs/audit-2026-06-04c/, shared/docs/audit-2026-06-04b/, shared/docs/AUDIT-2026-06-03.md, and shared/docs/AUDIT-2026-06-01.md finds only Windows-side decode-bomb findings (ENG-10/38/71 in Rust tagging.rs/deep_analyze.rs; a WinUI DecodePixelWidth preview nit) — nothing on the macOS scan-path PDF render. One minor correction that does not change the verdict: the "x3 pages" reads as a simultaneous multiplier, but the page loop is sequential with the CGContext scoped per iteration, so peak per-file footprint is ~1 page buffer (+ makeImage CGImage + Vision OCR working set); total work per file is 3 pages, and the dominant multiplier is the 14-32 concurrent files, which is correctly traced. OOM-kill consequence is plausible as stated: death occurs in the worker before the TaggedFile reaches DBWriter, so no failed=1 row is written and every rescan re-hits the same PDFs.
verdict.experiment: Not required (confirmed by static trace). If hardware validation is desired: generate a 36x48in (2592x3456pt) vector PDF (e.g. via CGContext PDF drawing), place 20 copies in a folder, scan it on a Mac while sampling `Hardware.residentMB()` — expect resident to jump by multiple GB (~143 MB x min(workerCap, 20) concurrent renders) vs a flat baseline with letter-size PDFs.

confirmed.title: macOS parallelStreamingDownload orphans multi-GB '.fileid-staging' part files forever on process death — no startup/next-run sweep exists, and each retry uses a fresh UUID
confirmed.line: StreamingDownload.swift:180-194; engine kill path: FileIDEngineMain.swift:147; callers: DeepAnalyze.swift:131 (via VLMDownloader.swift:147), CLIPModelInstaller.swift:267, ArcFaceModelInstaller.swift:98
confirmed.evidence: Parts are staged next to the destination under a hidden dir keyed by a per-run UUID: `let runID = UUID().uuidString` (line 180), `.appendingPathComponent(".fileid-staging", isDirectory: true)` (line 186), `stagingDir.appendingPathComponent("\(runID)-part-\(i)")` (line 189). The ONLY cleanup is `func cleanupParts() { for u in partURLs { try? FileManager.default.removeItem(at: u) } }` (192-194) — scoped to THIS run's UUIDs and only reachable while the process lives. Repo-wide grep: '.fileid-staging' appears nowhere else — no startup sweep, no stale-part GC. The engine dies without running any of this: `Darwin._exit(0)` on stdin EOF (FileIDEngineMain.swift:147), which is exactly what happens when the user quits the app while VLMDownloader.fetchRepo is mid-download. macOS has no resume (unlike Windows .part reuse), so orphaned parts are pure waste.
confirmed.trigger: User starts the Qwen2.5-VL 7B install (~5-7 GB of safetensors) from Deep Analyze, then quits the app at 80% (or the app crashes / power loss): engine gets stdin EOF -> _exit(0), leaving ~4-5 GB of '<uuid>-part-N' files in ~/Documents/huggingface/models/<repo>/.fileid-staging/. Relaunch + retry mints a NEW runID, re-downloads everything, and never touches the old parts. Same shape for app-side CLIP/ArcFace installs killed by app quit.
confirmed.impact: Silent, unbounded disk leak in a Finder-hidden dot-directory: a few interrupted multi-GB installs strand tens of GB; subsequent installs then fail the installers' own 2x free-space preflight ("Not enough free space", CLIPModelInstaller.swift:143) or genuinely fill the disk, with no user-discoverable cause and no UI to reclaim the space.
confirmed.suggestedFix: At the top of parallelStreamingDownload, delete every entry in stagingDir that doesn't carry the current runID (the dir is dedicated to FileID staging, so this is safe), or sweep entries older than 24h; remove stagingDir itself after cleanupParts when empty. Optionally add a launch-time sweep next to the model roots.

verdict.reasoning:
Set out to refute and could not — every load-bearing link survives independent trace at HEAD (5e5289c).

Mechanics verified:
1. Staging exactly as cited: /Users/adamnolle/Desktop/Code/FileID/platforms/apple/shared/Sources/FileIDShared/StreamingDownload.swift:180 (`let runID = UUID().uuidString`), :185-186 (stagingDir = dest.parent/.fileid-staging), :188-190 (`<runID>-part-<i>`). Completed parts are moved out of URLSession temp into stagingDir (:326-327), so they persist on disk independent of the process.
2. Cleanup is exclusively in-process and run-scoped: `cleanupParts()` (:192-194) iterates only this run's `partURLs` and is invoked only on the error/fallback/checksum/success paths of the live call (:213, 221, 228, 251, 259, 268, 273). Nothing on disk records the runID; a new call mints a fresh UUID and can never match old parts.
3. No sweep exists anywhere: repo-wide grep for "fileid-staging" hits exactly one file — the creation site. No startup GC, no age-based sweep, and `cleanupParts` never even removes the (then-empty) stagingDir on success.
4. Kill paths confirmed: engine hard-exits via `Darwin._exit(0)` on stdin EOF (FileIDEngineMain.swift:147) AND via the parent-death watchdog polling getppid()==1 every 5 s (:34-41) — both skip all Swift cleanup. App-side installers die with the app process the same way. Crash/power-loss trivially also orphans.
5. Callers confirmed: VLMDownloader.swift:147 (engine, dest = ~/Documents/huggingface/models/<repo>/<file>, called from DeepAnalyze.swift:131), ArcFaceModelInstaller.swift:98 (dest directly in the app models dir), CLIPModelInstaller.swift:267. None of these locations is OS-purged (user Documents / Application Support, not temporaryDirectory).
6. Kill-attempts that failed: no VLM uninstall/delete flow exists (DeepAnalyzeViews.swift only checks the `.fileid-installed` sentinel); CLIP/ArcFace `uninstall()` remove only the final model dirs, not staging; no test pins any sweep (Tests grep empty); macOS genuinely has no part-level resume — VLMDownloader resumes only at whole-file granularity (size+sentinel/hash skip, VLMDownloader.swift:86-97), so old parts are pure waste. The free-space-preflight impact quote is real (CLIPModelInstaller.swift:142-144 "Not enough free space"; ArcFaceModelInstaller.swift:82-85).

Not a duplicate: audit-2026-06-04c E16 ("Parallel→simple fallback orphans up to 12 stale .part-NN files", engine-deep-audit.json:524) is the WINDOWS downloader.rs bug with a different mechanism (fallback-path orphan, where parts otherwise persist by design for resume); R0-findings.json and shared/docs/AUDIT-2026-06-03.md contain nothing about macOS .fileid-staging or process-death part orphans.

Two overstatements in the finding text, neither verdict-changing: (a) "re-downloads everything" is wrong for VLMDownloader — fetchRepo skips completed files, so a retry restarts only the interrupted file; per-kill orphaned bytes ≈ the completed parts of the one in-flight shard (still multi-GB for 7B safetensors), and accumulation across repeated kill/retry cycles is genuinely unbounded since each run strands a fresh UUID set. (b) For CLIPModelInstaller the dests sit inside a `.clip-staging-<UUID>` root (CLIPModelInstaller.swift:148-151) whose `defer` cleanup also dies with the process and which likewise has no sweep — same leak shape, ~250 MB scale, with .fileid-staging nested inside.

Severity: medium is right. Trigger is mundane (quit the app mid-install — nothing blocks quitting during a 5-7 GB download), mechanism is certain, the leak is silent, Finder-hidden, and has no reclamation path; but it causes no data loss/corruption, is bounded per-incident by one shard's size, and is manually recoverable. Suggested fix (sweep stagingDir entries not matching the current runID at the top of parallelStreamingDownload, since the dir is FileID-dedicated) is consistent with the verified structure and would also want a `.clip-staging-*` sweep in CLIPModelInstaller for full coverage.
verdict.experiment: Not required (verdict is confirmed), but the on-hardware repro is: start the Qwen2.5-VL install from Deep Analyze on a Mac, force-quit the app mid-safetensors-shard, then `ls -la ~/Documents/huggingface/models/<repo>/.fileid-staging/` — expect stranded `<uuid>-part-N` files; retry the install and confirm the old UUID's parts remain after completion.

confirmed.title: Windows generateVideoThumbnail has no concurrency bound end-to-end: each command is an unbounded tokio::spawn + spawn_blocking Media Foundation extract holding a full-res frame (up to 192 MB) — scrolling a cache-cold video folder piles up 100+ concurrent extracts
confirmed.evidence: Dispatch: `CommandPayload::GenerateVideoThumbnail(payload) => { let sink_c = sink.clone(); tokio::spawn(async move { ...handle_generate_video_thumbnail... }) }` (main.rs:832-837) — no semaphore, unlike the scan pipeline's vision_sem/clip caps. Handler: `tokio::task::spawn_blocking(move || crate::shell::video::keyframe_25pct(&p))` (thumbnail.rs:40) — tokio's blocking pool allows 512 threads. Per-extract memory: `const MAX_VIDEO_PIXELS: u64 = 64_000_000` then `let mut rgb = vec![0u8; w*h*3]` (video.rs:198-214) = up to 192 MB per frame, plus an open MF SourceReader (D3D video-decode contexts). App side dedupes per (path,mtime) only and fires without any in-flight cap from the fast drain loop: `bool firstRequest = _requestedVideo.Add(key); if (firstRequest) { ... _ = EngineClient.Instance.GenerateVideoThumbnailAsync(req.Path, req.ModifiedAt); }` (ThumbnailService.cs:243-247); for videos RenderAsync returns null cheaply (no shell call), so the drain loop fires the next request within milliseconds. Prior records cover this path's redaction (R0 M18 follow-up, since fixed) and the 20s timeout — never its concurrency.
confirmed.trigger: Fresh install (or cleared thumbs.cache) + scroll through a video-heavy library folder: 150-300 .mov/.mp4 tiles each L1+L2-miss and fire one engine command within a few seconds. Each MF open+seek+decode takes 1-10 s (worse on the NAS corpus), so arrival rate x duration yields 100+ concurrent extracts, each pinning a decoded RGB frame (4K = ~25 MB, 8K = ~100 MB) plus a hardware decoder context.
confirmed.impact: On the 4 GB MemoryTier::Low target the engine transiently commits 2.5 GB+ of frames plus decoder contexts: paging collapse / engine OOM ('Engine crashed' 3-strike path). On any box the burst exhausts GPU video-decode contexts so extracts start failing — and `_requestedVideo` 'intentionally KEEPS the key' (ThumbnailService.cs:257-258), so those tiles show placeholders for the rest of the session.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Engine-side: gate handle_generate_video_thumbnail behind a small static tokio Semaphore (2-4 permits) around the spawn_blocking, mirroring the scan pipeline's GPU caps. Optionally app-side: cap in-flight video requests (e.g. 8) and queue the rest, so the 20 s timeout isn't consumed by queue wait.

Attempted refutation failed — every link of the chain verifies at HEAD, and no guard, cap, or prior record kills it.

1. /Users/adamnolle/Desktop/Code/FileID/platforms/windows/src/engine/src/main.rs:832-837 — `CommandPayload::GenerateVideoThumbnail` dispatches via bare `tokio::spawn` with no semaphore, no single-flight guard, no busy-bounce. Contrast verified: the only Semaphores in the crate are downloader.rs:46-48 (HTTP, 8 permits) and pipeline/tagging.rs:761-762 (vision_sem/clip_sem) — the on-demand thumbnail path has nothing. Adjacent arms show the codebase knows the pattern (RunFaceClustering single-flights via compare_exchange at 799-810); this arm just lacks it.

APP SIDE (all cited code confirmed):
5. ThumbnailService.cs:227-261 — `_requestedVideo.Add(key)` dedupes per (path,mtime) per session only; first request fires `_ = EngineClient.Instance.GenerateVideoThumbnailAsync(...)` fire-and-forget (line 247). No in-flight counter, no SemaphoreSlim anywhere in the file. EngineClient.Commands.cs:769-770 is a straight SendCommandAsync — no throttle.

The drain loop fires fast for videos: RenderAsync (lines 496-556) for a video does File.Exists + L2 disk-cache read (miss on fresh install / cleared cache) then returns null at the VIDEO_SHELL_SKIP branch (551-556) WITHOUT any shell call; DrainAsync (188-197) registers the pending TCS and immediately continues — it never awaits the engine round-trip. So one cache-cold scroll converts realized video tiles into engine commands at near disk-stat speed.
7. Placeholder permanence confirmed: lines 256-258 — "The dedupe set intentionally KEEPS the key so we don't re-hammer the engine for a video it couldn't extract this session." Burst-failed extracts stay placeholders all session.

NOT A DUPLICATE: shared/docs/audit-2026-06-09-merge/R0-findings.json covers this path only for redaction (M18 follow-up — since fixed: ThumbnailService.cs:246/251/287 now wrap in PathRedactor.Redact) and the Swift schema-mirror gap; greps for concurren/unbounded/semaphore/OOM/burst hit only downloader, tagging EP caps, and bulk-action races. audit-2026-06-04c/ and AUDIT-2026-06-03.md predate the engine video-thumbnail feature; their only thumbnail items are ThumbnailDiskCache sweep, DecodePixelWidth caps, and the OCR COM-init finding (which mentions commands/thumbnail.rs:40 only as a thread-reuse aside). This substitutes an arguably worse failure: 100+ concurrent software 4K/HEVC decodes saturate every core and multiply internal frame allocations — the memory/paging mechanism is unchanged.
(b) Concurrency magnitude: per-tile CTS cancellation on recycle (LibraryView.xaml.cs:608-612) can drop requests still queued when a tile scrolls out, and a fast flick may skip realizing some tiles — but the drain loop outruns recycling for the cheap video path, and once a command reaches the engine nothing cancels it (no cancel command exists for this path). "100+" requires the slow-extract end (NAS — exactly the project's G:\TrueNAS verification corpus); local-SSD libraries land at dozens of concurrent extracts, which on the 4 GB Low tier is still a multi-GB transient commit plus a blocking-pool flood that starves the engine's other spawn_blocking work.

SEVERITY: medium is correct. Transient resource-exhaustion burst with plausible engine OOM into the terminal 3-strike crash state plus session-sticky placeholder fallout, on a trigger (cache-cold scroll of a video-heavy folder) that is common for this product's target corpus — but no data loss/corruption, recoverable by restart, and gated on cold caches. Suggested fix (2-4 permit static Semaphore around the spawn_blocking, mirroring vision_sem) is sound and minimal.
verdict.experiment: On-hardware confirmation (next UAT pass): clear %LOCALAPPDATA%\FileID\thumbs cache, open a G:\TrueNAS folder with 150+ videos, scroll end-to-end while watching the engine process in Task Manager — expect commit spike of 1.5 GB+ and 50+ blocking threads (Process Explorer thread count), and after the burst, count tiles stuck on placeholder (VIDEO_ENGINE_TIMEOUT lines in app.log vs thumbnailGenerated events).

confirmed.title: Windows Low-tier predecode 'memory budget' counts frames at an assumed 24 MB while MAX_DECODED_PIXELS admits 150 MB frames, and the decoder pool is not tier-clamped — 40-50 MP libraries pin ~2.5-3.8 GB of read-ahead on the 4 GB target
confirmed.line: tagging.rs:701-712 (budget), 690-691 (decoder pool), 1018 (send_blocking holds frame), 1134 (50 MP cap); commands/scan.rs:272-273 (Low-tier worker clamp)
confirmed.evidence: `const PREDECODE_BUDGET_MB: usize = 256; const TYPICAL_FRAME_MB: usize = 24; // ~8 MP RGB8; let budget_cap = PREDECODE_BUDGET_MB / TYPICAL_FRAME_MB;` and `MemoryTier::Low => budget_cap.max(1)` (tagging.rs:701-710) — the bound is 10 SLOTS, not bytes. The per-frame ceiling is `MAX_DECODED_PIXELS` = 50 MP (tagging.rs:1134), which the code itself prices at '~150 MB RGB8' (tagging.rs:1882). The comment claims 'Per-frame pixels are already capped at MAX_DECODED_PIXELS, so this also tightens the pathological-frame ceiling' (699-700), but 10 x 150 MB = 1.5 GB read-ahead alone. Additionally `let decoder_count = ((topo.p_cores + topo.e_cores) as usize).clamp(2, 12);` (690-691) is NOT MemoryTier-clamped, and each decoder thread holds its fully decoded PreDecoded while blocked: `if tx.send_blocking(item).is_err()` (1018). Low tier clamps only workers (min 6, scan.rs:272-273) and the CLIP pool. Prior records cover the paused-state ~256 MB shape at TYPICAL frames (R0 preexisting) and the tier wiring itself (perf-mem sweep) — neither records the frame-vs-byte unit error under high-MP input.
confirmed.trigger: A 4 GB MemoryTier::Low machine (4-8 cores) scans a modern high-MP photo library — Canon R5 (45 MP), Nikon Z7 (45.7 MP), drone panoramas: all under the 50 MP cap, so every frame decodes to ~130-145 MB RGB8. Steady state: 10 channel slots (1.3-1.5 GB) + 4-8 decoder threads each blocked on send holding one frame (0.5-1.2 GB) + 6 workers' in-flight frames (~0.8 GB) ≈ 2.5-3.5 GB of pixel buffers, vs the documented ~256 MB design budget.
confirmed.impact: The explicit Low-tier memory fix is exceeded ~10x exactly on the constrained target: commit blows past RAM into the pagefile, the machine thrashes/freezes for the whole scan, throughput collapses, and on commit-limited boxes (small/disabled pagefile) the unguarded `to_rgb8()` allocations abort the engine -> 'Engine crashed' mid-scan, recurring on every rescan of the same library.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Make the predecode bound byte-weighted: wrap the channel in a byte-budget semaphore (acquire rgb.len() before send, release when the worker drops the frame), and clamp decoder_count under MemoryTier::Low (e.g. .min(4)). Cheap alternative: compute per-file slots from actual decoded size (slots = ceil(rgb_len / 24MB)) against the same 256 MB budget.

verdict.reasoning: Independent trace at HEAD confirms every load-bearing link. (1) tagging.rs:701-714: budget_cap = 256/24 = 10 and MemoryTier::Low => budget_cap.max(1), so the predecode channel is bounded at 10 ITEMS with no byte accounting anywhere in the crate (no rgb.len() weighting exists). (2) The only per-frame gate is MAX_DECODED_PIXELS = 50_000_000 (tagging.rs:1883, self-priced at ~150 MB RGB8 at line 1882); decode_image_sync_imagecrate returns full-res to_rgb8().into_raw() (1144-1146) with no downscale before the channel, so the comment at 699-700 claiming the budget 'tightens the pathological-frame ceiling' is wrong by ~6x per slot: 10 x ~134-150 MB = 1.34-1.5 GB vs the ~256 MB design intent recorded in STATE.md ('Pre-decoded RGB read-ahead byte-budgeted (~256 MB)', commit c07f93e/5196252). (3) decoder_count = (p+e).clamp(2,12) at tagging.rs:690-691 has no memory_tier() consult (verified: the only Low clamps are workers .min(6) at commands/scan.rs:272-274 and the ML session pool ->1 at tagging.rs:388-390); each decoder thread blocked in tx.send_blocking(item) (1018) owns one full decoded frame, and run_decoder_thread (885-1021) has no pressure gating. The abort claim is consistent: decode/to_rgb8 allocations are infallible (handle_alloc_error -> abort, NOT caught by the catch_unwind at 1112), and the adjacent pre-read explicitly uses try_reserve_exact to avoid exactly this class (910-914), confirming the decode allocations are unguarded by omission. AUDIT-2026-06-03.md and audit-2026-06-04b/c) finds no record of the slot-vs-byte unit error or the unclamped decoder pool. Minor wording nits in the finding (scan.rs clamp is .min(6) i.e. at-most-6; the tier pool clamp covers the whole ML pool, not just CLIP) do not affect the verdict. verdict.experiment: Not required (confirmed by static trace), but the decisive on-hardware check is: on a 4 GB Windows VM (or any box with <8 GB available so memory_tier()==Low), scan a corpus of ~200 x 45 MP JPEGs (8192x5464, e.g. resampled test images) and sample engine private working set via 'Get-Process fileid-engine | Select WS,PM' every 5 s; expect plateau >2.5 GB and pagefile thrash, vs ~0.5 GB after applying a byte-weighted (rgb.len()) predecode semaphore + decoder_count.min(4) under Low.

confirmed.trigger: Engine crashes (e.g. MLX exit SIGSEGV) — app shows the auto-respawn error pill but the sidebar stays interactive. Plausibility is strong — a crash-looping engine (corrupt library) is precisely when users click wipe, and the 4 s/16 s backoff windows (or indefinite .crashed state) are clickable. NOT a duplicate: R0-findings.json titles contain no such finding (wipeAndRescan appears only incidentally in H2/ReadStore/IPC-risk text); grep for wipeAndRescan/stdinPipe over audit-2026-06-04c/* and BUGS.md is empty; AUDIT-2026-06-03.md's wipe entries are the Windows commands/wipe.rs truncate-during-scan bug — different platform and mechanism. Suggested fix direction (handle stdinPipe==nil in wipeAndRescan by deleting directly since the engine is already down, and make send() report failure) is consistent with the verified structure.
verdict.experiment: Not needed (confirmed by static trace). If runtime proof is desired on the user's Mac: run the app, kill -9 the fileidd/FileIDEngine pid 2-3 times to enter the 4 s/16 s backoff (or 4 times for budget exhaustion), click 'Wipe library + rescan' and confirm within the window, then verify ~/Library/Application Support/FileID/fileid.sqlite mtime/inode is unchanged while a scan starts, and app.log shows no 'exit: clean' wipe pass between the click and the next .ready.

confirmed.title: Windows: C7 fix incomplete — per-row Cancel during engine cold start is still silently lost; the app-side cancel mark is erased by the very prewarm dispatch it should cancel (surviving branch (a) of R0 confirmed #7)
confirmed.evidence: Commit 580e213's C7 fix closed only the cancel-ALL degradation ('per-row Cancel can no longer degrade to cancel-ALL: InstallAll pre-stamp sets CurrentModelKind ...; CancelModelAsync(null) is a logged no-op'). The lost-cancel half of the recorded finding survives: WelcomeSheet.xaml.cs:404-407 sets `slot.Message = "Cancelling…"` and calls `Svc.CancelModelAsync(slot.CurrentModelKind)`; CancelModelAsync routes to `EngineClient.Instance.CancelPrewarmAsync(k)` (ModelInstallerService.cs:459-460); CancelPrewarmAsync sets `_prewarmCancelledKinds[modelKind] = 1` then `return SendCommandAsync(new CancelPrewarmCommand(modelKind))` (Commands.cs:626-628) — and SendCommandAsync hard-faults when `State != LifecycleState.Ready` ('Engine not ready ... ABORTED', Commands.cs:46-51), swallowed by WelcomeSheet.SafeRunAsync. Meanwhile ModelInstallerService.PrewarmAsync is still parked in `WaitForReadyAsync(WaitForReadyTimeout)` (up to 75 s, ModelInstallerService.cs:643). When the engine reaches Ready it resumes and calls EngineClient.PrewarmModelAsync, whose first act is `_prewarmCancelledKinds.TryRemove(modelKind, out _)` (Commands.cs:511-512, comment: 'A fresh prewarm means the user wants downloads') — but this prewarm was REQUESTED BEFORE the cancel. The engine never saw the cancel (send faulted), its per-kind flag map is irrelevant, and the download proceeds.
confirmed.trigger: First-run onboarding: welcome sheet appears while the engine is cold-starting (the code itself budgets up to 75 s). User clicks 'Install all' (pre-stamp now stamps CurrentModelKind, so the row IS cancel-able per C7), then clicks Cancel on the 6.1 GB Qwen row within the cold-start window. The CancelPrewarm IPC never reaches the engine; the app-side cancel mark is cleared at PrewarmModelAsync dispatch; the row flips 'Cancelling…' → 'Queued — starting download…' and the multi-GB download runs to completion.
confirmed.impact: User-visible broken cancel in the canonical onboarding path: cancelling a multi-gigabyte download does nothing — bandwidth and disk are consumed despite explicit cancellation, with a misleading 'Cancelling…' acknowledgment. This is the exact symptom R0 #7 documented as branch (a); the C7 remediation commit closed only branch (b).
confirmed.suggestedFix: In EngineClient.PrewarmModelAsync, do NOT unconditionally clear `_prewarmCancelledKinds[modelKind]`; instead, have ModelInstallerService.PrewarmAsync check `IsPrewarmCancelled(modelKind)` after WaitForReadyAsync resumes and before sending PrewarmModel — if marked, reset the slot (ResetForRetry) and return without dispatching. Alternatively queue the CancelPrewarm command for replay at Ready instead of letting SendCommandAsync fault. Clear the per-kind mark only on an explicit user Retry/Install action for that slot.

verdict.reasoning: Attempted refutation failed — every link verifies statically at HEAD (1e5cab3). CHAIN: (1) Welcome sheet launches at Content.Loaded (MainWindow.xaml.cs:134-139), before engine Ready on first run; cold-start budget up to 75 s is the code's own (WaitForReadyTimeout, ModelInstallerService.cs:65). (2) InstallAllAsync pre-stamps slots Downloading + CurrentModelKind=PrimaryKindFor(slot) (ModelInstallerService.cs:359-372, the C7 fix) and immediately fires TryInstallAsync → slot.InstallAsync → PrewarmAsync(kind), which parks at WaitForReadyAsync (line 643); DeepVlm's installAction calls PrewarmAsync(_deepVlmModelKind) (lines 158-168). (3) Downloading rows show a live Cancel (WelcomeSheet.xaml.cs:190); HandleAction sets Message="Cancelling…" and calls Svc.CancelModelAsync(slot.CurrentModelKind) (397-407) — non-null post-C7, so the per-kind path is taken (ModelInstallerService.cs:459), not the null no-op. (4) CancelPrewarmAsync(kind) sets _prewarmCancelledKinds[kind]=1 then SendCommandAsync (Commands.cs:626-628), which hard-faults on State != Ready (Commands.cs:46-51); WelcomeSheet.SafeRunAsync swallows the fault (469-479). The engine never receives the CancelPrewarm. (5) At Ready, PrewarmAsync resumes with NO IsPrewarmCancelled check anywhere in lines 643-678, skips reset (Status==Downloading, line 666), and calls EngineClient.PrewarmModelAsync, whose first act is _prewarmCancelledKinds.TryRemove(modelKind) (Commands.cs:511-512) before dispatching PrewarmModel. The app-side mark only ever gated the stall guard (Commands.cs:578/583) — it never aborts a download — and even that residue is erased. The 6.1 GB download proceeds despite "Cancelling…". NO KILLERS: no command queue/replay at Ready; CancelPrewarmAsync does not await readiness; nothing flips slot state on Cancel that would gate the resumed dispatch; git log shows no commit after 580e213 touched any of these files (only SECURITY.md + an IPC-schema test touched Windows paths). FIX-SCOPE VERIFIED: 580e213's C7 diff touches only ModelInstallerService.cs (pre-stamp + CancelModelAsync(null) no-op) — exactly branch (b); its commit message itself claims only "per-row Cancel can no longer degrade to cancel-ALL". Branch (a) (lost cancel on cold start) survives in per-kind form: the same mark-set → send-faults → mark-cleared-at-dispatch mechanism, now via _prewarmCancelledKinds instead of _prewarmCancelAllRequested. NOT A DUPLICATE: R0-findings.json #7 records the pre-fix two-branch finding, but 580e213 presents C7 as remediated among "All fixes" of the 7 confirmed findings, and no doc (grep "C7" in STATE.md/DECISIONS.md/NEXT.md: zero hits) records the surviving branch (a) as a known residual or deferral. The pre-fix trigger (null kind → cancel-all) no longer exists at HEAD; the surviving per-kind trigger is a live, unrecorded defect — an incomplete-fix finding, not a re-report. AUDIT-2026-06-03.md lines 225-226 cover distinct, already-fixed engine-side races (prewarm_cancelled wedging the slot at "Cancelling…"; per-model cancel lost in the spawn→register window — both engine-running scenarios); audit-2026-06-04c TRIAGE has only the unrelated E4 VLM-timeout cancel. One non-substantive narrative nit: the "Cancelling…" → "Queued — starting download…" caption flip is driven by the engine's post-dispatch Queued/progress event, not by PrewarmAsync itself (which skips its message rewrite because Status==Downloading); the substance — cancel silently lost, multi-GB download runs to completion — is exact. Severity stays medium: user-visible broken cancel in the canonical onboarding path with a misleading acknowledgment and multi-GB bandwidth/disk waste, but recoverable (a second Cancel click once the engine is Ready goes through the working per-kind path), no data loss, no crash. The suggested fix direction is sound: check IsPrewarmCancelled in ModelInstallerService.PrewarmAsync after WaitForReadyAsync resumes (before dispatch), or replay the CancelPrewarm at Ready instead of letting SendCommandAsync fault; do not unconditionally clear the mark at PrewarmModelAsync dispatch for a prewarm requested before the cancel.
verdict.experiment: Not required for the verdict (the chain is fully static), but for on-hardware confirmation: cold-boot a first-run install, click "Install all" within ~5 s of the welcome sheet appearing, immediately click Cancel on the Qwen row, and watch the debug log for "[IPC OUT] CancelPrewarm ABORTED — Engine not ready" followed (at engine Ready) by "[INSTALL] EngineClient.PrewarmModelAsync('qwen2_5_vl_7b')" and download progress events advancing past "Cancelling…" to completion.

confirmed.title: Windows: sentinel-write failure exits in prewarm.rs strand the install slot at ~99.9% 'Downloading' forever — sentinel_* error kinds are not routed by ModelInstallerService.HandleEngineError and no terminal progress event is emitted
confirmed.line: prewarm.rs:503-556 (three error returns), 558 (1.0 emit unreachable), 299 (fraction capped .999); ModelInstallerService.cs:899-912 (filter), 717-720 (watchdog); ModelSlot.cs:182-188
confirmed.evidence: handle_prewarm_model's sentinel block emits `kind: "sentinel_dir_create_failed"` (prewarm.rs:508), `"sentinel_write_failed"` (528), and `"sentinel_rename_failed"` (544) and `return`s on each (518/538/554) BEFORE the final fraction-1.0 ModelDownloadProgress at 558; all in-download progress events are capped at `fraction.min(0.999)` (299). App side: HandleEngineError's isInstallError filter (ModelInstallerService.cs:900-912) matches only model_download_failed / download_tls_pin_failed / zip_extract_failed / pack_not_available / unknown_model / models_dir_unavailable / `kind.StartsWith("prewarm_")` — the sentinel_* kinds match nothing (`grep -rn "sentinel_" FileID.App` returns zero handler hits), so `if (!isInstallError) return;` drops them and `slot.Fail()` never runs. ModelSlot.Apply can't rescue it either: its 100%-without-sentinel branch logs 'staying Downloading; a follow-up sentinel check will resolve' (ModelSlot.cs:182-187) but no 100% event ever arrives and no follow-up check exists. The no-progress watchdog is one-shot and already disarmed: `if (slot.LastProgressAt > sentAt || _lastAnyProgressAt > sentAt) return;` (ModelInstallerService.cs:720) — progress events flowed for the whole download. These error kinds predate all recorded audits (introduced in b518c50) and appear in no prior finding list.
confirmed.trigger: User installs Qwen2.5-VL 7B (6.1 GB) on a tight disk. The download itself succeeds (resumable parts survive), consuming the last free space; the sentinel tmp write at prewarm.rs:522 then fails with disk-full (or antivirus/ACL blocks the `.sentinels` dir create at 505). The engine emits sentinel_write_failed and exits the handler with no 1.0 event. The app drops the error; the slot sits at 'Downloading… 99%' with stale bandwidth.
confirmed.impact: The welcome-sheet/Settings row spins at ~100% forever with no Retry button (Failed is never set). Because Clip/Arcface/RamPlus/DeepVlm gate IsBusy and AllInstalled (ModelInstallerService.cs:540-549), a stuck slot wedges the Install-all gate and onboarding auto-dismiss for the whole app session (the Reset() comment documents exactly this blast radius). The engine's actionable message ('downloaded but not registered as installed; try again') never reaches the row. Recovery requires an app restart, after which the slot reads NotInstalled despite all bytes being on disk.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Either (engine) make the failure kinds routable — reuse an already-routed kind (e.g. prefix them `prewarm_sentinel_*` so the existing `StartsWith("prewarm_")` matches, treating them as Fail not cancel) — or (app) add the three sentinel_* kinds to isInstallError in HandleEngineError so slot.Fail(error.Message) runs. Adding them app-side is the minimal fix and preserves the engine's good per-kind messages.

verdict.reasoning:
Independent end-to-end trace confirms every load-bearing claim, and two additional "safety nets" I hunted for as potential refuters both turn out to be dead for these kinds — strengthening the finding.

ENGINE SIDE (platforms/windows/src/engine/src/commands/prewarm.rs, verified at HEAD): all in-download progress events are clamped `fraction.min(0.999)` (line 299). The sentinel block (503-556) emits `sentinel_dir_create_failed` (508, return 518), `sentinel_write_failed` (528, return 538), `sentinel_rename_failed` (544, return 554) — each `return` precedes the only fraction-1.0 emit at 558. So on any sentinel failure, no event with fraction >= 1.0 is ever sent for that model_kind. `registry::sentinel_path` (models/registry.rs:540-542) returns Some for every model whose models_dir resolves, so the block is not cfg-gated or skippable in the trigger scenario.

APP SIDE: EngineClient routes the Error event to `LastError` (EngineClient.cs:1180); the sentinel_* kinds are NOT in `IsNonFatalWarningKind` (EngineClient.Commands.cs:147-167), so they land in LastError as claimed. ModelInstallerService.HandleEngineError's isInstallError filter (ModelInstallerService.cs:900-912) matches exactly model_download_failed / download_tls_pin_failed / zip_extract_failed / pack_not_available / unknown_model / models_dir_unavailable / StartsWith("prewarm_") — none of the three sentinel_* kinds match, so line 913 `if (!isInstallError) return;` drops them and `slot.Fail()` never runs. A repo-wide grep for the three kind strings outside prewarm.rs returns ONLY shared/docs/DECISIONS.md:1685 — no app handler exists anywhere.

GUARDS HUNTED, ALL DEAD: (1) ModelSlot.Apply's 100%-without-sentinel branch (ModelSlot.cs:171-188) is unreachable — no >=1.0 event arrives — and its "follow-up sentinel check" does not exist (SeedFromSentinels/Refresh's SeedSlot explicitly skips Downloading slots, ModelInstallerService.cs:589-595). (2) The service no-progress watchdog (694-747, NoProgressTimeout=60s not 30s — trivial detail error in the finding) is one-shot, scheduled at IPC send, and disarmed by `slot.LastProgressAt > sentAt || _lastAnyProgressAt > sentAt` (720) since the download streamed progress for its whole duration. (3) A second guard the finding missed — EngineClient.StartPrewarmStallGuardAsync (EngineClient.Commands.cs:522-621), which exists precisely to surface wedged installs — explicitly RETURNS when `LastError.ModelKind == modelKind` (592-596), on the comment's assumption that the error "already routed"; for sentinel_* kinds that assumption is false, so the net self-disarms. Even if it fired, its `model_install_stalled` kind is also absent from the isInstallError filter, so it could never flip the slot to Failed either. (4) User Cancel cannot recover: WelcomeSheet's Downloading->Cancel sends CancelPrewarm, but the engine's handler (main.rs:534-538) only sets a per-kind flag; `prewarm_cancelled` is emitted only from inside an active handle_prewarm_model, which already returned — so no event arrives and the slot stays Downloading. (5) IsBusy/AllInstalled gating confirmed at ModelInstallerService.cs:540-549: a permanently-Downloading slot wedges IsBusy true and AllInstalled false for the session.

INTENT REGRESSION: DECISIONS.md:1685 documents that these three kinds were added "so the welcome row stops spinning with a clear message" — the app-side filter was never updated to route them, so the documented intent is broken.

DUPLICATE CHECK: grep for sentinel_dir_create_failed / sentinel_write_failed / sentinel_rename_failed, isInstallError, HandleEngineError, "staying Downloading" etc. across shared/docs/audit-2026-06-09-merge/R0-findings.json + TRIAGE.md, audit-2026-06-04c/*.json, audit-2026-06-04b/*.json, AUDIT-2026-06-03.md, AUDIT-2026-06-01.md, BUGS.md: zero hits for this issue. The only HandleEngineError mentions are in an unrelated 06-04c off-thread-PropertyChanged finding. The kinds were introduced in b518c50 (2026-05-15, verified via git show), predating none of the routing fixes. Not a duplicate.

MINOR TEMPERING (does not refute): the engine's actionable message is not 100% invisible — SidebarEngineStatus.xaml.cs:60-73 flips the engine-health indicator unhealthy and surfaces LastError.Message in its tooltip/flyout. But it never reaches the install row, no Retry appears, the welcome sheet stays wedged, and restart resets the slot to NotInstalled exactly as claimed. Severity stays medium: rare trigger (disk-full/AV-ACL exactly at sentinel-write time after a successful multi-GB download), but a full-session wedge of the install/onboarding UX with restart as the only recovery.
verdict.experiment: Not required (confirmed by static trace), but a decisive headless repro exists: in FileID.App.Tests, drive ModelInstallerService.HandleEngineError (or extract the isInstallError predicate like IsNonFatalWarningKind was made internal for tests) with EngineError kind="sentinel_write_failed", modelKind="qwen2.5-vl-7b" while the DeepVlm slot is Downloading at Fraction 0.999 — assert slot.Status flips to Failed; today it stays Downloading. Engine-side: run prewarm with a read-only Models dir (deny-ACL on .sentinels) after pre-seeding all model files; observe sentinel_write_failed emitted and no fraction-1.0 ModelDownloadProgress in the stdout transcript.

confirmed.title: Windows: Intel OpenVINO auto-install failure leaves the Accelerator slot stuck on 'Queued — starting download…' — the deliberate error swallow assumes pseudo-Installed, but the engine's unconditional Queued event already flipped the slot to Downloading
confirmed.evidence: On Intel, CudaAutoInstaller.TryInstallOpenVinoPack fires `EngineClient.Instance.PrewarmModelAsync("ort_openvino_x64")` directly (CudaAutoInstaller.cs:141), bypassing ModelInstallerService.PrewarmAsync — so NO no-progress watchdog is scheduled and CurrentModelKind is never stamped. The engine immediately emits the unconditional 'Queued — starting download…' fraction-0 event (prewarm.rs:131-140); HandleProgress routes `ort_openvino_x64` to the Accelerator slot (SlotFor, ModelInstallerService.cs:771-774) and ModelSlot.Apply unconditionally sets `Status = ModelInstallStatus.Downloading` (ModelSlot.cs:188), replacing the Intel pseudo-Installed state. When the pack download then fails (registry entry exists at registry.rs:384, but hosting is listed as not-done in platforms/windows/CLAUDE.md — a 404 is the documented expectation: 'the download 404s gracefully and Intel stays on DirectML', CudaAutoInstaller.cs:115-116), the engine emits model_download_failed with model_kind=ort_openvino_x64, and HandleEngineError deliberately returns: 'Don't alarm the user with a red Failed card; log and leave the Accelerator slot on its DirectML (pseudo-Installed) state' (ModelInstallerService.cs:944-951) — but the slot is Downloading at that point, not pseudo-Installed. Nothing heals it: the watchdog never armed, SeedFromSentinels' Accelerator branch only upgrades to Installed when a sentinel exists (574-580), UpdateAcceleratorForVendor only re-fires on a fresh Info event, and post-C7 the kind-less Cancel button is a no-op (CancelModelAsync(null), 453-457). Only ModelInstallerService.Reset() on an engine respawn flips it to Failed. The R0 #7 verdict mentioned this row only as a cancel-crossfire aggravator; the stuck-Downloading-on-failure path was never recorded.
confirmed.trigger: Any Intel-GPU machine, default settings (DisableAutoInstallOpenVino off): engine reaches Ready, CudaAutoInstaller silently dispatches the OpenVINO prewarm; the welcome sheet/Settings Accelerator row flips from 'Intel GPU — running on DirectML…' (Installed) to Downloading on the Queued event; the pack download 404s (unhosted) or fails on network; the error is swallowed; the row stays 'Queued — starting download…' for the rest of the session.
confirmed.impact: Every Intel user sees a perpetually-downloading GPU Acceleration row during onboarding and in Settings — uncancelable (kind-less Cancel no-ops) and unretryable — replacing the accurate 'DirectML is already optimal' message. Misleads users into waiting on a download that already failed; persists until an engine restart or app relaunch. (Functional scanning is unaffected — DirectML still runs.)
confirmed.suggestedFix: In the OpenVINO swallow branch (ModelInstallerService.cs:947-951), restore the slot instead of returning blind: if Accelerator.Status == Downloading, re-run UpdateAcceleratorForVendor("intel") (or set Status=Installed + the DirectML message + Fraction=1.0) so the row returns to its pseudo-Installed state. Longer-term, route the background OpenVINO install through PrewarmAsync so it gets the kind stamp and watchdog like every other install.

verdict.reasoning:
CONFIRMED with one load-bearing correction: the bug mechanism survives every refutation attempt, but the "404 is the expected/default outcome" premise is wrong, which narrows the trigger from "every Intel user" to "any Intel user whose OpenVINO auto-download fails".

Chain independently re-traced at HEAD (5e5289c):
1. CudaAutoInstaller.cs:141 (TryInstallOpenVinoPack) dispatches EngineClient.Instance.PrewarmModelAsync("ort_openvino_x64") directly, bypassing ModelInstallerService.PrewarmAsync — so no slot no-progress watchdog is scheduled (ModelInstallerService.cs:691 path never runs) and Accelerator.CurrentModelKind is never stamped. Verified.
2. The engine emits the unconditional fraction-0 "Queued — starting download…" event at handler entry (prewarm.rs:131-140, before the sentinel/registry checks). Verified.
3. ModelInstallerService is guaranteed subscribed before this arrives: EngineClient touches ModelInstallerService.Instance.Reset() at spawn start (EngineClient.cs:422, 835), and the Info event (ReadyEvent, EngineClient.cs:1046) has already set the Intel pseudo-Installed state via UpdateAcceleratorForVendor (ModelInstallerService.cs:238-246). HandleProgress routes ort_openvino_x64 → Accelerator (SlotFor, 771-774; not in IsAutoInstallerOnly, 787-792), and ModelSlot.Apply line 188 unconditionally flips Status=Downloading (fraction 0.0 < 1.0 path), replacing pseudo-Installed. Verified.
4. On download failure the engine emits model_download_failed / download_tls_pin_failed / zip_extract_failed with model_kind=ort_openvino_x64 (prewarm.rs:402-408, 460-466, 475-481) and emits NO further progress for that kind. HandleEngineError's OpenVINO swallow (ModelInstallerService.cs:947-951) keys on error.ModelKind and returns without touching the slot — which is Downloading, not pseudo-Installed as the comment assumes. Verified.
5. No healer exists — I hunted for guards the finding missed and found one candidate it never cites, which also fails to heal: EngineClient's detached prewarm stall guard (EngineClient.Commands.cs:518, 522-621) wakes ≤120 s later, sees LastError.ModelKind == "ort_openvino_x64" and returns silently (lines 592-596); even if LastError were overwritten first and the guard fired, its "model_install_stalled" kind is not in HandleEngineError's isInstallError list (900-912), so the slot still wouldn't change. SeedFromSentinels/Refresh only upgrades Accelerator on a real sentinel (574-580; SeedSlot is never called for Accelerator). UpdateAcceleratorForVendor re-fires only on a fresh Info, which is published once per spawn (ReadyEvent). The visible Cancel button (WelcomeSheet ShowAcceleratorButton returns Visible for Downloading) pre-flips Message to "Cancelling…" (WelcomeSheet.xaml.cs:397-407) then calls CancelModelAsync(null) which is a logged no-op (ModelInstallerService.cs:453-457) — so a cancel attempt actually wedges the row at "Cancelling…" forever, an aggravator the finding understates. Only an engine respawn heals (Reset → Failed → fresh Info → pseudo-Installed), and the respawn re-arms CudaAutoInstaller (EngineClient.cs:1058), re-entering the cycle if the failure is persistent.

REFUTED SUB-CLAIMS (severity-relevant, not verdict-changing):
- "the pack download 404s (unhosted) ... a 404 is the documented expectation": registry.rs's ort_openvino_x64 entry explicitly says "Hosted on HF (CI-allowlisted)" with a pinned sha256, the registry test pins it to huggingface.co, platforms/windows/CLAUDE.md's not-done item is the ORT *CUDA* pack hosting (not OpenVINO), and a live HEAD of https://huggingface.co/Web-World-Wide/OpenVINO/resolve/main/ort-openvino-win-x64-1.22.0.zip returns 200. The CudaAutoInstaller doc-comment about graceful 404 is stale defensive prose.
- "Every Intel user sees a perpetually-downloading row": overstated. The happy path (download succeeds → fraction 1.0 + sentinel → Installed) works. The stuck state requires a failed download — but that population is real and systematic, not exotic: download_tls_pin_failed is also swallowed (the swallow keys on ModelKind, not kind), so every Intel machine behind corporate HTTPS-inspection proxies hits this on EVERY session (TLS pinning landed in Phase 3); transient network failures during onboarding's multi-GB Install All contention also qualify.
- "the error is swallowed" is true at slot level, but not fully invisible: model_download_failed is not in IsNonFatalWarningKind (EngineClient.Commands.cs:147-167) so it lands in LastError and SidebarEngineStatus renders a persistent red engine-error pill — which simultaneously defeats the swallow's stated "don't alarm the user" goal while the row still claims to be downloading.

NOT A DUPLICATE: R0-findings.json's 12 findings were enumerated; #7 (per-row cancel null-kind → cancel-ALL) cites the kind-less OpenVINO Downloading row only as a pre-C7 cancel-crossfire aggravator, not the swallow-leaves-slot-stuck-Downloading failure path. audit-2026-06-04c's OpenVINO mentions are all ep_guard/EP-variant/BGE-INT8 issues; AUDIT-2026-06-03.md item 15 is ep_guard reenable. None records this defect.

Severity stays medium: onboarding-visible, recurs every session for the proxy/flaky-network Intel population, in-session unrecoverable, cancel attempt wedges the row at "Cancelling…", but zero functional/data impact (DirectML scanning unaffected). The suggested fix (restore pseudo-Installed state in the swallow branch via UpdateAcceleratorForVendor("intel"), and/or route the auto-install through PrewarmAsync) is sound.
verdict.experiment: Static trace was conclusive; for on-hardware confirmation: on an Intel-GPU Windows box (or with engine GpuVendor forced to "intel"), delete %LOCALAPPDATA%\FileID\Models\.sentinels\ort_openvino_x64.installed, block huggingface.co (hosts file or firewall) to force the download failure, launch FileID, and observe the welcome-sheet/Settings GPU Acceleration row flip from "Intel GPU — running on DirectML…" to a permanent "Queued — starting download…" with a Cancel button that wedges it at "Cancelling…".

confirmed.title: Migration chain forked at v14: macOS 'v14_fts_sync_triggers' installs FTS triggers into the shared DB while Windows keeps manual ocr_fts/doc_fts maintenance — a macOS-touched library deterministically fails every Windows scan with 'database disk image is malformed' (reproduced)
confirmed.line: migrations.rs:5-11 (canonical-list claim), 42 + 81-85 (v14_files_kind_scanned_index + NOTE); /Users/adamnolle/Desktop/Code/FileID/platforms/apple/engine/Sources/FileIDEngine/Storage/Database.swift:362-384 (v14_fts_sync_triggers); /Users/adamnolle/Desktop/Code/FileID/platforms/windows/src/engine/src/pipeline/dbwriter.rs:449-487 (manual FTS writes); /Users/adamnolle/Desktop/Code/FileID/platforms/windows/src/engine/src/scan_session.rs:363-375 (error propagation)
confirmed.evidence: migrations.rs:5-11 declares the lockstep invariant — 'Identifiers MUST match the canonical strings byte-for-byte: ... v13_face_verification_anchors, v14_files_kind_scanned_index' — and migrations.rs:81-83 demands 'NOTE: macOS must register an identical `v14_files_kind_scanned_index` identifier with equivalent SQL for cross-platform DB parity.' But macOS's actual v14 is a DIFFERENT migration: Database.swift:362 `m.registerMigration("v14_fts_sync_triggers")` creating `ocr_text_ai/_ad/_au` + `doc_text_*` triggers (Database.swift:366-383) with the contract 'The DBWriter no longer writes ocr_fts directly (the AFTER INSERT trigger does it)' (Database.swift:360-361). Windows still maintains the same external-content index MANUALLY: dbwriter.rs:450-452 `"DELETE FROM ocr_fts WHERE rowid = ?1"` then ocr_text DELETE+INSERT then dbwriter.rs:461-463 `"INSERT INTO ocr_fts (rowid, text) VALUES (?1, ?2)"` (doc_fts mirror at 470-487). Triggers are schema objects stored IN the DB file, so on a round-tripped DB Windows's manual statements and macOS's triggers both fire. Empirically reproduced with sqlite3 (exact v14 trigger SQL + exact dbwriter sequence): on a consistently-indexed row, `DELETE FROM ocr_fts WHERE rowid=1` followed by `DELETE FROM ocr_text WHERE file_id=1` makes the AD trigger issue the FTS5 'delete' command for already-removed postings → 'Runtime error: database disk image is malformed (11)', and the aborted statement then makes the re-insert fail 'UNIQUE constraint failed: ocr_text.file_id'. The dbwriter propagates this with `?` (dbwriter.rs:453-455 .with_context) out of flush (dbwriter.rs:114-143) and `writer.run(...).await?` (scan_session.rs:363-375) aborts the whole scan. Each engine silently tolerates the other's unknown identifier (migrations.rs:92-104 checks only its own registry; GRDB DatabaseMigrator.swift:618 'Only known ids'), so the fork is invisible at open time. No record exists: DECISIONS.md/STATE.md/LOCKSTEP docs contain zero mentions of either v14 identifier (grep), and R0-findings.json examined the v12→v14 rename only as an intra-macOS GRDB-upgrade question ('benign') and macOS v14 only intra-platform ('DBWriter has zero manual ocr_fts writes left'). The apple CLAUDE.md still claims the schema is 'byte-faithful with the Windows engine so a library round-trips across platforms', and Database.swift:339-346 (v13) says the lockstep exists precisely 'so a face DB round-trips cross-platform'.
confirmed.trigger: User scans a library on the merged macOS build — v14 applies, permanently installing the six sync triggers into fileid.sqlite. They move the library DB to their Windows machine (the round-trip flow v12/v13 were explicitly engineered for). Windows engine opens it: migrations::apply ignores the unknown 'v14_fts_sync_triggers' row, applies its own 'v14_files_kind_scanned_index', no warning. Then ANY re-process of a file with existing OCR/doc text — a modified document, a moved file healed onto its old row, or a `startScan(rescan:true)` force-reprocess after a model install (Windows honors the flag per scan_session.rs:161-163) — runs the dbwriter sequence with ocr_stage_ran=true: manual ocr_fts DELETE removes the postings, the ocr_text DELETE fires macOS's ocr_text_ad trigger whose FTS5 'delete' hits the now-empty doclist → SQLITE_CORRUPT (11) → flush errors → entire scan fails. The transaction rolls back, so every retry fails identically — scanning is permanently broken for the migrated library. Meanwhile files first-ingested on Windows get double-indexed (trigger insert + manual insert), skewing bm25 ranking.
confirmed.impact: The headline cross-platform contract ('a library round-trips across platforms') is broken in the worst direction: after a Mac→PC migration, every scan that touches a previously-indexed file aborts with a corruption error; there is no in-product recovery except wipeLibrary, which destroys person names and user tags. The fork is silent (no decision recorded, both engines open the DB without complaint) and also poisons the migration namespace going forward: both platforms now claim slot 'v14' with different content, so the recorded plan to 'mirror the v14 (kind, scanned_at) index migration into the apple engine' (NEXT.md:148-151) can no longer be executed as written.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Reconcile and record. Preferred: add Windows 'v15_fts_sync_triggers' with byte-identical trigger SQL and delete the manual ocr_fts/doc_fts statements from dbwriter.rs (also closes the previously-recorded-benign Windows dangling-postings class); add macOS 'v15_files_kind_scanned_index' so both chains re-converge with both v14s + both v15s registered on both platforms; update the migrations.rs:5-11 canonical list and append the rationale to DECISIONS.md. Minimum stopgap if triggers are not wanted on Windows: at writer open, detect `SELECT 1 FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='trigger' AND name='ocr_text_ai'` and either DROP the triggers in a new migration or skip the manual FTS statements when present. Add a round-trip regression test: apply macOS's trigger DDL to a Rust-built DB, run a dbwriter re-process, assert no SQLITE_CORRUPT.

verdict.reasoning: Independent trace confirms every link, including an empirical reproduction on the engine's exact bundled SQLite. (1) The fork is real at HEAD: migrations.rs:42 registers v14_files_kind_scanned_index and its doc header (lines 5-11, 81-82) declares it the canonical v14 macOS must mirror, while Database.swift:362 registers a different v14 (v14_fts_sync_triggers) installing six ocr_text/doc_text sync triggers (366-383); DBWriter.swift:460 confirms macOS removed its manual ocr_fts writes. (2) No guard kills it: migrations::apply (92-106) ignores unknown grdb_migrations rows; the Windows engine contains zero trigger DDL or trigger detection (grep), and SETUP_PRAGMAS (db/mod.rs:18-29) sets nothing (e.g. trusted_schema=off) that would suppress foreign-trigger execution — so both engines open the forked DB silently and the macOS triggers fire under Windows writes. (3) Windows still maintains FTS manually: dbwriter.rs:221-242 prepares DELETE FROM ocr_fts WHERE rowid=?1 / INSERT INTO ocr_fts(rowid,text) (+doc mirror), executed at 449-487 when ocr_stage_ran/doc_stage_ran. (4) Reproduced on the exact bundled SQLite: Cargo.lock pins rusqlite 0.31/libsqlite3-sys 0.28 (bundled SQLite 3.45.0); a minimal rusqlite 0.31 program applying the macOS trigger DDL to a consistently indexed row then running the dbwriter sequence fails deterministically at the ocr_text DELETE with SqliteFailure(DatabaseCorrupt, extended 267, "database disk image is malformed") — same result on sqlite3 3.51.0. (5) Propagation confirmed: dbwriter.rs:453-455 `?` → flush → run() `?` (114/122/129/143) → scan_session.rs:375 writer.run(...).await? aborts the session; tx rollback makes retries identical; rescan:true honored at scan_session.rs:161-163 so the documented force-reprocess remedy is itself a guaranteed trigger. (6) Not a duplicate: R0-findings.json (239-240, 263) examines v14_fts_sync_triggers only intra-macOS (GRDB rename benign; the "zero manual ocr_fts writes" grep was apple-scoped); audit-2026-06-04c/engine-deep-audit.json:628 is the opposite Windows-only dangling-postings defect premised on no triggers existing; shared/docs/AUDIT-2026-06-03.md:236 mentions only the Windows (kind,scanned_at) v14. No doc records the fork; NEXT.md:148-151 still plans to mirror "the v14 (kind, scanned_at) index" into the apple engine, which the taken v14 identifier now blocks. Minor evidence nit not affecting the verdict: the claimed follow-on "UNIQUE constraint failed: ocr_text.file_id" is a CLI-repro artifact — the Rust code uses INSERT OR REPLACE (dbwriter.rs:219) and never reaches the insert because the DELETE error propagates first. Severity stays high (not critical) per the repo's own calibration: R0 downgraded the content-hash drift to medium expressly because it had "no cross-platform DB exposure" — this finding IS the cross-platform exposure case, deterministically and unrecoverably breaking the explicitly engineered round-trip contract (v12/v13 NOTEs, Database.swift:339-346, apple CLAUDE.md) and silently forking the append-only migration namespace; it is not critical because the product is pre-release, the trigger requires a manual cross-machine DB move, and the underlying data (ocr_text/doc_text, tags, persons) remains intact and recoverable via an FTS rebuild.
