verdict.experiment: Minimal rusqlite 0.31 (bundled SQLite 3.45.0, matching the engine's Cargo.lock) program: create ocr_text + external-content ocr_fts (porter unicode61), install the exact macOS v14 trigger DDL, insert one row (ai trigger indexes it), then run the exact Windows dbwriter sequence in one transaction — DELETE FROM ocr_fts WHERE rowid=1 followed by DELETE FROM ocr_text WHERE file_id=1. Result: the second statement fails with SqliteFailure(DatabaseCorrupt, extended_code 267, "database disk image is malformed"), deterministic; same outcome on system sqlite3 3.51.0.

confirmed.title: Deep Analyze model load requires a live huggingface.co round-trip even when the model is fully installed and sentinel-verified — offline Macs can never run Deep Analyze, and every load is non-download network egress
confirmed.evidence: ensureLoaded step 1 unconditionally calls `try await VLMDownloader.shared.fetchRepo(repo: kind.sourceRepo, ...)` (DeepAnalyze.swift:131) — the only short-circuit is the in-process cache `if container != nil, loadedKind == kind` (DeepAnalyze.swift:103), which is empty on every engine spawn. fetchRepo's FIRST action is a network call: `let files = try await listRepoFiles(repo: repo, revision: revision)` (VLMDownloader.swift:57) via `session.data(for: req)` to `https://huggingface.co/api/models/<repo>/tree/<revision>` on an `.ephemeral` session (no cache, VLMDownloader.swift:182-194). The `.fileid-verified-<revision>` sentinel — written by this branch's 36e09fa precisely to attest "a fully verified fetch" — is only consulted AFTER the listing succeeds (`let sentinelValid = FileManager.default.fileExists(atPath: verifiedSentinel.path)`, VLMDownloader.swift:71-72), so it never spares the network call. Step 4 then loads with `HubApi(downloadBase: documentsHF, useOfflineMode: true)` (DeepAnalyze.swift:162) — the code's own intent is local-only loading once files are present.
confirmed.trigger: User installs Qwen2.5-VL 7B (4.3 GB) while online; `.fileid-verified-<revision>` + `.fileid-installed` sentinels are written; UI shows the model installed. User later goes offline (plane, air-gapped studio, HF outage/DNS failure — plausible for a privacy-focused no-telemetry app) and runs Deep Analyze. Fresh engine process → ensureLoaded → fetchRepo → listRepoFiles throws URLError → ensureLoaded rethrows (DeepAnalyze.swift:178-183) → DeepAnalyzeRunner emits `deep_load_failed: Could not load Qwen2.5-VL 7B…` + deepAnalyzeComplete(processed:0) (DeepAnalyzeRunner.swift:144-155). Same wedge via the prewarm path (FileIDEngineMain.swift:312).
confirmed.impact: A headline feature is unusable offline despite a complete, hash-verified local install — the persisted verification state the sentinel exists to provide is ignored at the exact moment it matters. Additionally, every online Deep Analyze run / model prewarm emits a huggingface.co API request that downloads nothing, sitting awkwardly against the PRIVACY.md contract that the only egress is user-initiated model downloads (this fires on every analyze of an already-installed model).
confirmed.suggestedFix: In fetchRepo, check the `.fileid-verified-<revision>` sentinel BEFORE listRepoFiles: if it exists (optionally plus a cheap local check that the model dir is non-empty), return success immediately with progress(1,0,0). Alternatively, catch listRepoFiles network failures and fall back to the local load when both sentinels exist. Either way, add the offline-with-installed-model case to the test corpus.

verdict.reasoning: Independent trace confirms every load-bearing claim. (1) DeepAnalyze.swift:131 — ensureLoaded unconditionally awaits VLMDownloader.shared.fetchRepo; the only short-circuit is the in-process cache at line 103 (container != nil), empty on every fresh engine spawn, and the surrounding do/catch (lines 178-183) rethrows with no offline fallback. (2) VLMDownloader.swift:57 — fetchRepo's first awaited op is listRepoFiles, an HTTPS GET to https://huggingface.co/api/models/<repo>/tree/<revision> on a freshly-created .ephemeral URLSession (lines 181-194; in-memory-only cache, new per call — nothing can serve it offline). The .fileid-verified-<revision> sentinel is only read at lines 71-72, AFTER the listing succeeds, so it never spares the network call despite being written (commit 36e09fa, verified on this branch) to attest a fully verified install. ModelManifest.vlmPin is a compiled-in local table, so the revision is known locally — the suggested sentinel-first fix is feasible. (3) Exactly two callers, both confirmed: DeepAnalyzeRunner.swift:128 (offline throw → deep_load_failed + deepAnalyzeComplete(processed:0), lines 137-156) and FileIDEngineMain.swift:312 (prewarm → prewarm_failed). No connectivity gating or alternate load path exists anywhere in app or engine (grep for NWPathMonitor/reachability: only comments). (4) App UI shows the model installed via the .fileid-installed sentinel (ModelInstallStatus in DeepAnalyzeViews.swift), so the offline-with-installed-model user experience is exactly as described. (5) PRIVACY.md:21 explicitly promises "After the model lands, that feature works fully offline" — directly contradicted in both directions: feature is unusable offline post-install, and a zero-download HF API round-trip fires on every first-load per engine process. Duplicate check: walked all findings in audit-2026-06-09-merge/R0-findings.json, all six audit-2026-06-04c JSONs + TRIAGE.md, and AUDIT-2026-06-03.md — nothing covers offline VLM load, fetchRepo/listRepoFiles, or macOS HF egress; keyword hits were unrelated (Windows downloader.rs, "tree", etc.). One mild overstatement in the finding's impact text: "every online Deep Analyze run" — runs after the first within one engine process hit the in-process container cache and skip the network; it is per-process-first-load and per-model-switch (still every app launch's first analyze and every prewarm). This does not change the verdict. Severity stays medium: a headline feature hard-fails offline despite a complete, hash-verified local install, contradicting the user-facing privacy/offline contract, but there is no data loss or security breach, the error is surfaced cleanly, and the workaround (be online once per app launch) exists. Files: platforms/apple/engine/Sources/FileIDEngine/Pipeline/DeepAnalyze.swift, .../VLMDownloader.swift, .../DeepAnalyzeRunner.swift, platforms/apple/engine/Sources/FileIDEngine/FileIDEngineMain.swift, shared/docs/PRIVACY.md.
verdict.experiment: Not required (verdict is confirmed by static trace). If on-hardware confirmation is wanted: install Qwen2.5-VL 7B online, verify .fileid-verified-<revision> exists, disable Wi-Fi, relaunch FileID, run Deep Analyze on one image — expect deep_load_failed with a URLError instead of a local load; re-enable network and observe the huggingface.co /api/models tree GET in a proxy despite zero bytes downloaded.

confirmed.title: Windows engine: truncate_to_max calls String::truncate at a fixed byte offset BEFORE the char-boundary fixup — panics on any >256 KiB non-ASCII document, silently killing decoder threads and dropping files from the scan
confirmed.line: doc_extract.rs:52-60 (panic site); tagging.rs:997 (call site, no catch_unwind), 719-721 (detached decoder threads); Cargo.toml:244-250 (panic=unwind keeps the process alive, so failure is silent)
confirmed.evidence: doc_extract.rs:52-58: `fn truncate_to_max(mut t: String) -> String { if t.len() > MAX_TEXT_BYTES { t.truncate(MAX_TEXT_BYTES); while !t.is_char_boundary(t.len()) { t.pop(); } } t }`. Rust's `String::truncate` is documented to PANIC "if new_len does not lie on a char boundary" — the `while !is_char_boundary` pop-loop placed AFTER it is dead code in exactly the case it was written for. The existing test `truncate_respects_char_boundary` (doc_extract.rs:368-374) passes only by luck: it uses 2-byte 'é' repeated, and MAX_TEXT_BYTES = 262144 is even, so the cut is always boundary-aligned; any 3-byte char (CJK, ₹, …) or odd alignment panics. Reachability: `extract()` is invoked directly at tagging.rs:997 inside `run_decoder_thread` with no catch_unwind (the only catch_unwind in the crate wraps the image codec, tagging.rs:1112); decoder threads are detached `std::thread::Builder::spawn` (tagging.rs:719-721) whose JoinHandles are dropped. Inputs reach the truncate >MAX: read_plain reads MAX_TEXT_BYTES+4 bytes (doc_extract.rs:77), and the docx/pptx/xlsx/pdf accumulators only `break` AFTER `out.len() > MAX_TEXT_BYTES` (lines 154, 217, 109), so they can exceed it by a whole text run.
confirmed.trigger: Scan a library containing one ordinary large non-ASCII text document — e.g. a 300 KB Chinese/Japanese/Russian .txt or .md, or a French/German .docx whose accumulated text exceeds 256 KiB — where byte offset 262144 of the extracted text falls inside a multi-byte char (~2/3 of the time for 3-byte scripts, ~1/2 for 2-byte). extract() panics in truncate_to_max; the decoder thread unwinds and dies (panic hook just logs to engine.jsonl). The file never reaches the PreDecoded channel → no files row, not marked failed → reprocessed (and re-panics) on every rescan. Each such doc permanently kills one of the [2,12] decoder threads for the rest of the scan; with decoder_count such docs, all decoders die, the raw channel loses all receivers, the pipeline winds down, and the scan reports completion having processed only a fraction of the corpus.
confirmed.impact: Silent catalog gaps: affected documents (and, once enough decoder threads die, arbitrarily many unrelated files) are missing from Library/search with no error surfaced — the scan 'completes'. Throughput degrades scan-over-scan in mixed corpora. The engine process survives (panic=unwind), so nothing alerts the user; this defeats the doc-text pipeline for exactly the non-English documents the BGE-accent finding (06-04c) was about, one stage earlier.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Clamp to a boundary BEFORE truncating: `let mut n = MAX_TEXT_BYTES; while !t.is_char_boundary(n) { n -= 1; } t.truncate(n);` (or `t.truncate(t.floor_char_boundary(MAX_TEXT_BYTES))` once stable). Fix the regression test to use a 3-byte char (e.g. '夏'.repeat(100_000)) so it actually pins the boundary case. Optionally wrap the per-file body of run_decoder_thread in catch_unwind so any future extractor panic marks the file failed instead of killing the thread.

verdict.reasoning: Independently traced and empirically reproduced. (1) Panic site verified: doc_extract.rs:52-60 calls String::truncate(MAX_TEXT_BYTES) BEFORE the char-boundary pop-loop; std's truncate asserts is_char_boundary(new_len), making the fixup dead code in the exact case it was written for. Compiled a byte-for-byte replica: "夏".repeat(100_000) (300 KB, 3-byte chars) panics with 'assertion failed: self.is_char_boundary(new_len)'; the existing test's "é".repeat(200_000) passes only because 262144 is even (262144 mod 3 = 1, so pure 3-byte content panics deterministically). The test pin at doc_extract.rs:368-374 is therefore illusory. (2) Reachability verified: discovery.rs:81-82 maps txt/md/docx/xlsx/pptx to FileKind::Doc; tagging.rs:996-999 invokes doc_extract::extract inside run_decoder_thread (fn at tagging.rs:885) — .ok().flatten() catches Err only, not panics, and the crate's sole catch_unwind in this path (tagging.rs:1112) wraps the image codec only. Inputs exceed MAX_TEXT_BYTES: the Doc pre-read (tagging.rs:956-965) buffers up to FULL_HASH_MAX_BYTES = 16 MiB (content_hash.rs:18) and read_plain returns the full lossy-decoded buffer; the path fallback reads MAX+4 bytes; the docx/pptx/pdf accumulators break only AFTER out.len() > MAX. (3) Silent failure verified: decoder JoinHandles are discarded via the `if let Err(e) = spawn_result` pattern (tagging.rs:719-730); Cargo.toml:244-250 deliberately keeps panic="unwind"; logging.rs install_panic_hook only logs to tracing (no abort). The file never reaches the PreDecoded channel, is never persisted nor marked failed (re-panics every rescan), and each such file permanently kills one of the [2,12] decoder threads for the remainder of the scan. (4) Duplicate check: grepped audit-2026-06-09-merge/R0-findings.json, all of audit-2026-06-04c/, AUDIT-2026-06-03.md, and BUGS.md for truncate/char-boundary/doc_extract — only CLIP-tokenizer L25, DB wipe truncate, and the BGE accent finding appear; this is not recorded anywhere. Severity high is appropriate: ordinary non-English documents (>256 KiB CJK txt/md or accented docx whose accumulated text exceeds the cap) silently vanish from the catalog, decoder capacity degrades cumulatively within a scan, and the engine process survives so nothing is surfaced to the user. The suggested fix (find the boundary before truncating + a 3-byte-char regression test) is correct.
verdict.experiment: Compiled and ran a byte-for-byte replica of truncate_to_max (rustc -O): "é".repeat(200_000) succeeded (len=262144, mirroring the existing test's alignment luck); "夏".repeat(100_000) panicked at the t.truncate(MAX_TEXT_BYTES) line with 'assertion failed: self.is_char_boundary(new_len)', exit code 101 — proving the boundary-fixup loop is dead code and the panic is real for 3-byte scripts (262144 ≡ 1 mod 3).

confirmed.title: Filename search is Unicode-normalization-sensitive on both platforms: NFC query never matches NFD-stored path_text (macOS on-disk names are NFD), silently returning zero results — directly violates the shared unicode-names corpus assertion
confirmed.line: ReadStore.swift:166, 175-184 (LIKE columns); platforms/windows/src/FileID.App/Services/ReadStore.cs:205-214 (same shape); DBWriter.swift:400 (stores file.url.path verbatim); shared/test-corpus/unicode-names/manifest.json:4-5 (the violated expectation)
confirmed.evidence: macOS ReadStore.swift:175 `OR path_text LIKE ? ESCAPE '\\'` (plus vlm_proposed_name/vlm_description/tags/persons LIKEs at 176-184) binds `"%\(escapedSearch)%"` with zero normalization — grep for precomposedStringWithCanonicalMapping/decomposedString across platforms/apple returns nothing. SQLite LIKE is a byte-wise comparison (ASCII-only case folding), so NFC 'café' (63 61 66 C3 A9) cannot match NFD 'café' (63 61 66 65 CC 81). macOS stores the enumerator's on-disk form: DBWriter.swift:400 writes `file.url.path` and Foundation file-creation APIs write names in canonical-decomposed (NFD) form, so GUI-created accented filenames are NFD in the DB while macOS keyboard input in the search field produces NFC. Windows ReadStore.cs:205 `OR f.path_text LIKE $like ESCAPE '\'` has the identical gap (no String.Normalize anywhere in FileID.App, grep confirmed) and bites on Mac/NAS/Dropbox-synced NFD-named files. The shared corpus manifest (unicode-names/manifest.json:4-5) explicitly requires: "searchable by 'café' regardless of query normalization". Only the FTS columns are immune (tokenize='porter unicode61' folds diacritics, Database.swift:108 / migrations.rs:180) — the filename/tag/person LIKE columns are not.
confirmed.trigger: macOS: any accented filename created via Finder/Preview/most apps (NFD on disk, e.g. the corpus case café-nfd.jpg) is scanned; user types 'café' (NFC) in the Library search box → the LIKE misses → file absent from results even though its tile is visible when the box is empty. Windows: same query against a library containing Mac-synced NFD names (NAS/iCloud/Dropbox) → same silent miss. Reverse direction (NFD query via paste vs NFC-stored name) fails symmetrically.
confirmed.impact: Filename search — a headline Library feature — silently returns zero results for accented/composed names for non-English-locale users; same gap applies to person-name search (persons.name LIKE) where names are user-typed in one form and pasted/retyped in the other. No error, no hint; users conclude the file isn't indexed.
confirmed.suggestedFix: macOS: normalize both sides to NFC — apply .precomposedStringWithCanonicalMapping to path_text (and person names) at every writer (DBWriter.insertOne, restructure apply, applyProposedName; APFS resolves either form so stored-NFC paths still open) and to the search string in ReadStore.files(). Windows: NTFS filenames must keep their on-disk bytes, so normalize at compare time instead — normalize the query to FormC and add an NFC-normalized shadow column (or register a custom collation/function) for path_text/person-name search written by the engine. Wire the unicode-names corpus case into both iterate harnesses as the regression gate.

verdict.reasoning: Every cited line verified at HEAD. macOS: ReadStore.swift:162-188 escapes only LIKE metacharacters and binds the raw query to `path_text LIKE ? ESCAPE '\'` (line 175) plus vlm_proposed_name/vlm_description/tags/persons LIKEs (176-184); DBWriter (engine/Sources/FileIDEngine/Storage/DBWriter.swift:400 — finding's line exact, directory is Storage/ not Database/) writes file.url.path verbatim into path_text. Windows: ReadStore.cs:187-215 is the identical shape (path_text LIKE $like at 205). Greps confirm zero Unicode normalization on either platform (no precomposedString*/decomposedString* in platforms/apple; no String.Normalize/NormalizationForm in FileID.App; none in the Rust engine). Kill attempts all failed: (1) no FTS rescue — ocr_fts/doc_fts are external-content over ocr_text/doc_text only (Storage/Database.swift:104-110, migrations.rs:176-181); filenames are not FTS-indexed, so the diacritic-folding unicode61 tokenizer never sees them; (2) no ICU — GRDB uses system SQLite, rusqlite is bundled, so LIKE is byte-wise with ASCII-only case folding and NFC 'é' (C3 A9) cannot match NFD 'e'+U+0301 (65 CC 81); (3) no test pin — FTSQueryTests.swift:80 only pins FTS quoting of 'café'; unicode-names is wired into neither iterate.sh nor iterate.ps1/scan_assertions.py; (4) the corpus contract is real — shared/test-corpus/unicode-names/manifest.json cases 1-2 require "searchable by 'café' regardless of query normalization" and create café-nfd.jpg at runtime. NOT a duplicate: R0-findings.json, audit-2026-06-04c/*, AUDIT-2026-06-03.md (plus audit-2026-06-04b and BUGS.md as extra diligence) contain no NFC/NFD/diacritic/normalization-search finding; commit 80bdd75's "search consistency" items (L18/L19) are debounce + failed=0 filtering, unrelated. Two accuracy notes that scope but do not kill the finding: (a) when the CLIP text encoder is installed and the query embeds, both platforms replace keyword search entirely with semantic ranking (LibraryView.swift:685-689; ClipSearchService.cs:222-243), so the LIKE path bites out-of-box without CLIP, for <3-char queries (macOS), on empty semantic results (macOS), or on embed failure (Windows) — but it is the ONLY filename-matching path that exists at all; (b) APFS is normalization-preserving rather than NFD-enforcing, so "Foundation file-creation APIs write NFD" is overstated, yet the failure is symmetric (NFC-vs-NFD in either direction) and mixed-provenance libraries plus the corpus's own NFD case guarantee the mismatch. The suggested fix direction (normalize-to-NFC at macOS writers + query, compare-time normalization on Windows, wire the corpus case into both harnesses) is consistent with the verified code structure.
verdict.experiment: Not needed (verdict is confirmed), but the decisive check would be: create café-nfd.jpg (0x65 0xCC 0x81 bytes) via the unicode-names harness, scan, then with no CLIP text encoder installed type NFC "café" in the Library search box on either platform and observe zero results while LIKE '%caf%' (unaccented prefix) still matches — equivalently, in sqlite3: SELECT 'cafe' || char(0x0301) LIKE '%café%' returns 0.

confirmed.title: Spotlight index retains AI captions, tags, and full paths of deleted/wiped files forever — SpotlightIndexer.wipe() has zero callers and indexAll never reconciles deletions
confirmed.evidence: makeItem() pushes `attrs.title = smart` (VLM smart name), `attrs.alternateNames = [URL(fileURLWithPath: r.path).lastPathComponent]`, `attrs.contentDescription = r.description` (VLM caption), `attrs.keywords = r.tags`, `attrs.contentURL = URL(fileURLWithPath: r.path)` into the system-wide CoreSpotlight index with no expirationDate. Detail.swift:30-37 calls `SpotlightIndexer.indexAll(...)` on every scan completion and every Deep Analyze completion — there is no opt-in setting (grep: zero 'spotlight' hits in AppSettings.swift/SettingsView.swift). `wipe()` is defined at lines 44-48 with the comment 'Exposed for a future "Delete my data" flow' and has zero call sites (repo grep: only the definition). No other file references CSSearchableIndex. indexAll only adds/updates by uniqueIdentifier 'fileid-<id>'; it never deletes items for rows that no longer exist.
confirmed.trigger: User runs Deep Analyze (captions like 'Mia at Beach' get indexed), then trashes sensitive photos via Cleanup (ReadStore.deleteFiles) or runs Settings wipe-and-rescan (EngineClient.swift:646 wipeAndRescan — deletes the DB, never touches Spotlight). The next indexAll only upserts current rows; the trashed/wiped files' items stay. After a wipe, autoincrement IDs restart so 'fileid-<id>' collisions overwrite low IDs but every leftover high-ID item keeps the old caption/path indefinitely.
confirmed.impact: AI-generated descriptions, smart names, tags, and full file paths of files the user explicitly deleted remain queryable in system-wide ⌘Space Spotlight forever — outside every data location PRIVACY.md's 'Where data lives' table lists, and not cleaned by scripts/wipe_local_state.sh. PRIVACY.md only discloses passive OS indexing ('whether or not FileID talks to it'), not this active CoreSpotlight push. Privacy regression that survives library deletion.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Call SpotlightIndexer.wipe() in wipeAndRescan and re-index after the fresh scan; on Cleanup trash / orphan sweep, call CSSearchableIndex.deleteSearchableItems(withIdentifiers: ["fileid-<id>"...]) (or have indexAll diff current IDs against the domain and delete the complement). Add a Settings toggle and disclose the active Spotlight integration in PRIVACY.md.

verdict.reasoning: Independent trace confirms every mechanical claim. (1) SpotlightIndexer.swift:27-40 indexAll only upserts via indexSearchableItems; no deletion/reconciliation logic exists anywhere — repo-wide grep for deleteSearchableItems hits only the wipe() definition at lines 44-48, which has zero call sites (comment says 'future Delete my data flow'). (2) makeItem (lines 94-103) pushes VLM smart name (attrs.title), real filename (alternateNames), VLM caption (contentDescription), tags (keywords), and full path (contentURL) into the system-wide CoreSpotlight index. (3) Detail.swift:30-37 fires indexAll unconditionally on every scan and Deep Analyze completion; Detail is mounted in MainWindow.swift:72; repo-wide grep confirms no Spotlight settings toggle. (4) EngineClient.swift:646 wipeAndRescan → deleteLibraryFiles (662-676) removes only SQLite/WAL/SHM/logs; ReadStore.deleteFiles (ReadStore.swift:56) deletes DB rows only; wipe_local_state.sh covers App Support/caches/defaults but not the CoreSpotlight app index — none touch Spotlight. (5) Post-wipe ID restart means 'fileid-<id>' collisions overwrite low IDs while leftover high-ID items keep old caption+path. (6) PRIVACY.md:55 discloses only passive OS indexing, contradicted by this active push. Not a duplicate: zero spotlight/CSSearchable hits in audit-2026-06-09-merge/R0-findings.json, audit-2026-06-04c/, audit-2026-06-04b/, AUDIT-2026-06-03.md, AUDIT-2026-06-01.md, BUGS.md, or STATE/NEXT/SHIP/DECISIONS. One overstatement: 'forever' — Apple documents that CSSearchableItems without an explicit expirationDate are auto-expired after a system-default interval (~30 days, OS-controlled, not contractual), so deleted files' items likely lapse eventually; could not be empirically timed (no Mac/Xcode in this env). That trims the title's rhetoric but not the substance: AI captions/tags/full paths of explicitly deleted or wiped files remain queryable in system-wide ⌘Space for a meaningful post-deletion window with no cleanup path, no opt-out, and no disclosure. Severity stays medium: real privacy regression in a privacy-centric product, but data is local-only (no egress) and retention is likely time-bounded by OS default expiration.
verdict.experiment: Not required for verdict; optional hardware check: on a Mac, run Deep Analyze, trash a captioned photo via Cleanup, then query its caption in ⌘Space immediately and again after 31+ days (or inspect `mdfind "kMDItemDescription == '<caption>'"`) to measure the actual retention tail vs the documented ~30-day default expiration.

refutedTitles: N1-mac-toctou: convertSymlinksToMoves: verified-symlink check-then-act ends in recursive FileManager.removeItem — a swap inside the race window the code's own comment declares in-scope gets a directory recursively and permanently deleted (no Trash); remove-then-move is also non-atomic

N7-unicode-paths: macOS restructure into an accented person folder writes NFC path_text while the disk gets NFD: the next rescan can't find the row, creating a permanent duplicate + ghost pair — and Cleanup then offers to 'trash the duplicate', which trashes the only physical copy

N7-unicode-paths: RTL-override (U+202E) filename spoofing is unmitigated in both UIs despite the shared corpus assertion, and macOS 'Open' has no SEC-9 allowlist — a name rendered as '…exe.jpg' can be opened/executed via NSWorkspace.shared.open

lows.title: Seeded lead scope confirmed: engine Restructure.apply is currently DEAD CODE on macOS (zero callers; applyRestructure IPC returns not_implemented_yet) but is the designated future applyRestructure implementation and carries the full uncorrected hazard set — fileExists/moveItem TOCTOU, unvalidated createDirectory, no symlink or root-containment checks (no root parameter), raw vlmProposed filename, and a collision-handling parity divergence vs Windows
lows.line: Restructure.swift:252-300 (apply: createDirectory 267-272, fileExists pre-check 274-278, moveItem 280), 207-219 (proposeAll raw vlmProposed at 210-211); FileIDEngineMain.swift:360-379 (dead IPC arm)
lows.evidence: apply() does `try fm.createDirectory(at: newURL.deletingLastPathComponent(), withIntermediateDirectories: true)` (268-269), then the non-atomic conflict probe `if fm.fileExists(atPath: newURL.path) { conflicts.append(p.newPath); skipped += 1; continue }` (274-278), then `try fm.moveItem(at: oldURL, to: newURL)` (280). Signature `apply(proposals:database:)` (252-255) takes no libraryRoot, so containment is impossible; there is no symlink check anywhere in the engine target (grep: zero isSymbolicLink/resolvingSymlinks hits under engine/Sources). proposeAll trusts `s.vlmProposed` raw: `newName = ext.isEmpty || ext == "_" ? p : "\(p).\(ext)"` (210-211) — safe only because both engines' writers slug-sanitize (DeepAnalyze.swift:450-484), an invariant apply-time code shouldn't lean on. REACHABILITY: grep over engine/app/shared/Tests finds zero callers of Restructure.apply or Restructure.proposeAll (only the definitions); the app plans+applies in-process (RestructureView), never imports FileIDEngine, and `.applyRestructure` dispatches to the grouped `not_implemented_yet` arm (FileIDEngineMain.swift:360-379). PARITY QUESTION (flagged, not decided): on collision this path skips and reports (`conflicts.append`, 274-277) where Windows restructure_apply uniquifies with a " (2)" suffix (unique_destination) — the cross-platform behavior diverges the moment this is wired. Note the live app sibling already fixed the fileExists TOCTOU pattern (RestructureView.swift:1384-1387); this engine sibling never received that fix.
lows.trigger: None today — the code is unreachable from the app and IPC. It goes live the moment Phase-5 parity work wires the applyRestructure IPC command to this implementation (the schema keeps the command symmetric across platforms and the dispatch arm's comment frames the stub as temporary): at that point every hazard above (symlink-ancestor root escape via createDirectory, TOCTOU conflict misclassification where a racing creation turns a 'conflict' into a generic 'failed', no containment) ships at IPC trust level, on the engine side where Windows required SEC-5 + SEC-7.
lows.impact: No user impact at current HEAD (dead code) — severity low for that reason alone. Latent impact if wired unchanged: engine-level moves that can escape the library root through symlinked ancestors, plus a silent cross-platform behavioral divergence on name collisions (macOS skip vs Windows disambiguate) that would make the same plan produce different libraries on the two platforms.
lows.suggestedFix: Either delete apply()/proposeAll() until the parity work lands (the app owns the macOS flow), or harden now so the wiring can't ship the hazards: add a libraryRoot parameter with resolved-path containment + pre/post symlink-ancestor walk (port of SEC-5/SEC-7), drop the fileExists pre-check in favor of catching EEXIST atomically (as RestructureView.swift:1384-1400 already does), run vlmProposed through FilesystemNameSafe.componentSafe at line 210 like the non-VLM branch at 213, and record an explicit DECISIONS.md entry resolving the skip-vs-uniquify collision parity question before wiring.

lows.title: macOS DBWriter keeps the scan running after a persistent SQLITE_FULL — every subsequent batch is dropped (with an error event each) while the full ANE/CLIP pipeline burns hours, and the session still ends 'completed'
lows.line: DBWriter.swift:185-225 (catch emits but never aborts), 134-147 (drain continues), 303-306 (SQLITE_FULL correctly non-transient)
lows.evidence: isTransient retries only `.SQLITE_BUSY || .SQLITE_LOCKED || .SQLITE_IOERR` (303-306) and the loop caps at 3 attempts — so SQLITE_FULL does NOT spin (good). But the catch path only logs + emits: `JSONLog.shared.error(ev: "db_write_failed", ...)` ... `case .SQLITE_FULL: userMessage = "Disk full — free space and re-run."` ... `await sink.emit(.error(...))` (197-224) and returns to drain(), which keeps committing every subsequent ~30-file batch (134-147). No coordinator.requestCancel(), no fatal flag, and processedTotal/failedTotal stay frozen (193-196 skipped), while markSessionFinal later records the session as finished.
lows.trigger: The volume hosting fileid.sqlite fills mid-way through a 50K-file scan (e.g. a concurrent multi-GB model download or Time Machine churn on a near-full boot drive). From that point, every batch commit fails with SQLITE_FULL: 1000+ db_write_failed events over the remaining hours, all Vision/CLIP/OCR work executes per file with nothing persisted.
lows.impact: Hours of compute and battery wasted after the failure point; the UI is spammed with repeated disk-full errors while the progress counter freezes mid-scan; the scan ultimately 'completes' with most files missing from the library, and the user must free space and rescan everything.
lows.suggestedFix: In commit()'s catch, track consecutive non-transient write failures; after N (e.g. 3), call coordinator.requestCancel() (or set a writerFatal flag the workers check) so the scan aborts promptly with the single actionable disk-full error already crafted at line 217.

lows.title: macOS ThumbnailService NSCache cost omits the Retina backing scale — the 256 MB totalCostLimit never engages; the real bound is countLimit 800 ≈ ~470 MB of bitmaps
lows.evidence: `c.countLimit = 800; c.totalCostLimit = 256 * 1024 * 1024` (18-19), but the request is made at screen scale `let scale = NSScreen.main?.backingScaleFactor ?? 2` (34) — a 192pt request yields a 384x384 px representation (~590 KB RGBA) — while the cached cost is computed from points only: `cache.setObject(image, forKey: key, cost: Int(size * size * 4))` = 147,456 (42). At 4x undercount, the 256 MB cost ceiling would only trip at a notional ~1 GB resident, so the effective bound is countLimit 800 x ~590 KB ≈ 470 MB on Retina — ~2x the declared '256 MB ceiling' (comment, line 19). No prior record (grep STATE/NEXT/DECISIONS + all audit JSONs for ThumbnailService/NSCache/totalCostLimit: none; file untouched since the Phase-0 commit).
lows.trigger: Scroll a large library grid on any Retina Mac (every shipping Mac) until 800 distinct tiles have been thumbnailed — routine browsing of a 50K-file library.
lows.impact: The app retains roughly double the intended thumbnail memory (~470 MB vs 256 MB), additive with the engine's scan working set — meaningful pressure on 8 GB Macs. Bounded (countLimit), so no runaway; correctness-of-bound only.
lows.suggestedFix: Cost with actual pixels: `cost: Int(size * scale * size * scale * 4)` (scale is already captured at line 34), or derive from the returned rep's pixel dimensions.

lows.title: Windows model install on a full disk is reported as a connection problem: ENOSPC's cause is hidden by anyhow Display, the hint says 'check your connection and click Retry', and Windows (unlike macOS) has no free-space preflight despite needing ~2x model size transient
lows.line: prewarm.rs:113-121 (hint), 387-404 (detail formatting); downloader.rs:969-970 (ENOSPC site), 716-745 + 783-785 (2x transient: concat while parts on disk); macOS contrast: CLIPModelInstaller.swift:143
lows.evidence: Disk-full surfaces at `tokio::io::AsyncWriteExt::write_all(&mut file, &chunk).await.context("writing range chunk")?` (downloader.rs:969-970). prewarm formats the detail with `format!("{label}: {err}")` (prewarm.rs:389) — anyhow's Display prints only the outermost context, so the user-visible text is 'writing range chunk' with the 'There is not enough space on the disk (os error 112)' source dropped. The kind/hint fork only special-cases TLS pinning; everything else gets `("model_download_failed", "Large model downloads can take several minutes — check your connection and click Retry. Downloads resume from where they stopped, so no progress is lost.")` (113-119). Repo grep: no DriveInfo/AvailableFreeSpace/GetDiskFreeSpace anywhere under platforms/windows, while the macOS installers preflight: `status = .installFailed("Not enough free space (need ~\(approxBytes * 2 / 1_048_576) MB).")` (CLIPModelInstaller.swift:143). Peak disk need is ~2x: the combined file is written while all 12 parts remain (downloader.rs:716-745); parts are deleted only after the rename (783-785). Prior downloader records (E16, H13, R0 stall-budget, '.part orphan on fallback') never cover ENOSPC classification or preflight.
lows.trigger: A small-SSD box with ~8 GB free installs Qwen2.5-VL 7B (6.1 GB): ranges fill the disk -> ENOSPC -> red pill 'Couldn't download ...: writing range chunk' + connection advice. Retry resumes the parts and fails again within seconds. Even after freeing 2-3 GB, the concat phase (needs another ~6 GB while parts persist) fails the same way.
lows.impact: User is steered into a connection-troubleshooting retry loop while the actual cause — a full disk, aggravated by ~6 GB of invisible .part files the app offers no way to clear — is never stated. Install appears permanently broken until the user independently discovers they need ~12 GB free.
lows.suggestedFix: In download_failure_kind_and_hint (or before it), inspect the error source chain for io::ErrorKind::StorageFull / os errors 39/112 and emit a dedicated kind ('model_download_disk_full') with copy stating how much space is needed and where the .part files live; format detail with `{err:#}` so the OS cause survives; add a preflight `expected_bytes * 2` free-space check against the destination volume before spawning ranges, mirroring macOS.

lows.title: Windows: DbWriter failure aborts ScanSession::run before the scan_sessions completion stamp — the row stays status='running' forever in Settings → Recent scans; GPU-death scans are stamped 'completed'
lows.evidence: ScanSession::run propagates a DbWriter error with `?` at line 375 (`let (total, failed) = writer.run(...).await?;` — DbWriter::run fails on flush errors, e.g. `tx.commit().context("commit batch")?`, dbwriter.rs:532). Everything after is skipped: `tick.abort()` (380), the post-drain notice (387-431), and the completion stamp `UPDATE scan_sessions SET completed_at = ?1, total_files = ?2, status = ?3` (443-446) — the 'running' row inserted at 143-155 is never finalized. The caller (commands/scan.rs:290-305) emits PhaseChanged(Failed) + a scan_failed error but touches no DB state. SettingsView.PopulateRecentScansAsync reads these rows directly (`FROM scan_sessions`, SettingsView.xaml.cs:97) for the Recent-scans list. Additionally, on the skipped-`?`-but-reached path the still-running tick task keeps emitting Progress(phase=Discovering) events for up to several seconds after the caller's PhaseChanged(Failed) (the pipeline unwinds only after tagger workers notice the dropped receiver), briefly flipping the sidebar back to 'Discovering…'. Separate inconsistency in the same block: the gpu-dead arm emits PhaseChanged(Failed) (449-464) but line 438 computes `final_status = if cancelled {"cancelled"} else {"completed"}` — a TDR-aborted scan is recorded as 'completed'. Prior audits recorded the GPU-death failed-flag stranding and the E3 notice race, but not the missing/incorrect scan_sessions stamps.
lows.trigger: Disk fills (SQLITE_FULL) or the DB file becomes unreachable mid-scan: a DbWriter flush errors, `?` exits run() before line 436. Or: GPU TDR mid-scan (coordinator.is_gpu_dead) — run() reaches the stamp and writes 'completed' for a Failed scan.
lows.impact: Settings → Recent scans permanently lists the failed scan as 'running' (or a TDR-aborted scan as 'completed'), misrepresenting library state to the user and to any future resume/re-scan affordance built on the status column; plus a few seconds of contradictory 'Discovering…' progress after the Failed banner.
lows.suggestedFix: Wrap the writer phase so the stamp always runs: capture `let outcome = writer.run(...).await;` then perform tick.abort() and the scan_sessions UPDATE (status = 'failed' on Err, 'failed' on is_gpu_dead, 'cancelled'/'completed' otherwise) before propagating the error with `outcome?`. Add 'failed' to the status vocabulary the Recent-scans renderer understands.

lows.title: macOS: ArcFace installer — a stale progress tick can overwrite the terminal status after cancel/failure, and refreshStatus deliberately preserves .downloading, leaving a permanent phantom 'Downloading…' row with a dead Cancel button
lows.evidence: runInstall's progress callback spawns an unstructured `Task { @MainActor [weak self] in ... self.status[kind] = .downloading(...) }` (101-116) for every tick (up to 10 Hz from URLSession's queue). The catch arms set the terminal status (`.missing("Cancelled.")` at 119, `.installFailed(...)` at 122/125/128) on the MainActor — but a tick task enqueued before the failure propagated can be scheduled AFTER the catch runs (the catch resumes via the higher-priority install task's continuation, which can jump ahead of a default-priority unstructured tick job on the MainActor), overwriting the terminal state back to `.downloading` with no install task alive (`tasks[kind]` was nilled at 63). Unlike CLIPModelInstaller.refreshStatus (which overwrites unconditionally and so self-heals), ArcFace's refreshStatus explicitly preserves the phantom: `if case .downloading = status[kind] { continue }` (53). `cancel(_ kind:)` is `tasks[kind]?.cancel()` (68) — a no-op on the nil task.
lows.trigger: User cancels the SFace download (or it fails mid-stream with multiple range parts in flight, so sibling parts' ticks are still draining while the error propagates): the catch sets .missing/.installFailed, then one last queued tick task runs and writes .downloading(fraction: …). Settings later calls refreshStatus, which skips downloading slots — the phantom persists.
lows.impact: The Settings face-model card shows a frozen 'Downloading… N MB' progress row for the rest of the session; its Cancel button does nothing (no task), and the install/retry affordance is hidden because the row believes a download is active. Heals only on app relaunch. CLIPModelInstaller shares the same stale-tick race (CLIPModelInstaller.swift:268-275) but self-heals on the next refreshStatus.
lows.suggestedFix: Guard tick application on liveness: have runInstall hold a per-run generation token (or check `tasks[kind] != nil` / a `finished` flag set in the catch) inside the spawned MainActor tick task before writing `.downloading`; and make ArcFace's refreshStatus only preserve .downloading when `tasks[kind] != nil`, otherwise overwrite from disk state like the CLIP installer does.

lows.title: No newer-DB/downgrade guard on either platform: both engines silently open a DB migrated beyond their registry and write with stale contracts — a pre-merge macOS build on a post-v14 DB hits the same proven FTS corruption error via the shared default DB path
lows.evidence: Database.swift:37 `try Self.migrator.migrate(pool)` — GRDB's plain migrate() never errors on a DB containing unknown applied identifiers: DatabaseMigrator.swift:618 builds `appliedMigrations` from 'Only known ids', so the 'database is already migrated beyond' fatalError (line 624) is unreachable in a downgrade and unknown identifiers are skipped by unappliedExecutions (lines 597-611); the engine never calls hasBeenSuperseded(). Windows mirrors this: migrations.rs:92-104 only SELECTs its own registered identifiers from grdb_migrations — rows ahead of the registry are never even read. Both engines share fixed DB paths across builds (Database.swift:41-47 `~/Library/Application Support/FileID/fileid.sqlite`). The stale-writer-contract harm is concrete, not hypothetical: the pre-merge origin/main macOS DBWriter (git show 4ff9789, lines 385-390) maintains ocr_fts manually — `DELETE FROM ocr_fts WHERE rowid = ?` + `INSERT INTO ocr_fts (rowid, text) VALUES (?, ?)` ('mirrors the Windows ocr_fts_delete') — the exact sequence empirically shown to raise 'database disk image is malformed (11)' against the v14 triggers (see the v14-fork finding's repro).
lows.trigger: Dev/QA Mac that has run the merged build (v14 triggers applied to the default-path DB) switches back to an origin/main checkout or an older kept FileID.app copy — `bash run.sh` is routine on this repo. The old engine opens the same fileid.sqlite, GRDB silently ignores the unknown 'v14_fts_sync_triggers' row, and the first rescan of any OCR'd file runs the old manual delete → cascade trigger 'delete' → SQLITE_CORRUPT error; the scan fails with no hint that the DB is from a newer build. The same silence applies to any future Windows downgrade (older registry simply ignores newer rows).
lows.impact: Downgrades produce confusing hard failures (or, for semantics-changing migrations like model resets/epoch flips, silent misbehavior) with no diagnostic pointing at the version mismatch — the error surfaces as DB corruption, the worst possible misdirection during the active multi-branch dev phase this repo is in, and there is no guard to add one later without someone noticing the gap. Exposure today is dev/QA builds (pre-release), hence low.
lows.suggestedFix: At open, detect ahead-DBs and refuse-with-message instead of writing: macOS — `if try pool.read(migrator.hasBeenSuperseded) { throw/emit kind: "db_newer_than_engine" }` before migrate(); Windows — after MIGRATION_TABLE_DDL, one `SELECT identifier FROM grdb_migrations` diffed against registry(), bail with a structured engine error naming the unknown identifier. Both apps already surface structured engine errors (db_open_failed path, FileIDEngineMain.swift:57-64), so the message reaches the user.

lows.title: One torn trash_log.json line containing a split multi-byte UTF-8 character permanently wedges restoreFromTrash for ALL batches and disables the 1024-line cap — read_to_string aborts the whole file instead of skipping the bad line
lows.evidence: read_batch: `let raw = std::fs::read_to_string(&path)?;` (trash_log.rs:94) — `read_to_string` returns Err(InvalidData) if ANY byte sequence in the file is invalid UTF-8, before the per-line skip logic (the `continue` on missing-HMAC at 101-104 and HMAC-mismatch at 107-110 never runs). trim_to_cap has the same whole-file read (`let raw = std::fs::read_to_string(path)?;`, trash_log.rs:65) and its failure is permanently swallowed (`trim_to_cap(&path).ok();`, trash_log.rs:59). serde_json::to_string emits non-ASCII path characters as raw UTF-8 (no \u escaping), and append writes the line via multi-fragment `writeln!(file, "{json}\t{mac}")` + `sync_all()` (trash_log.rs:52-55), so a power loss before the fsync completes (or an ENOSPC partial write — append's Err is only warned at bulk.rs:416-418 while the action still returns success) can persist a torn line cut mid-codepoint. handle_restore_from_trash propagates the Err (`trash_log::read_batch(&payload.batch_id)?`, trash.rs:17) into a failed BulkActionResult (bulk.rs:518-532). Nothing ever repairs or truncates the file.
lows.trigger: User with non-ASCII paths (e.g. C:\Users\René\Bilder\Café.jpg — common outside en-US) trashes a batch; power loss / hard crash / disk-full lands mid-append, leaving a torn line ending inside a multi-byte character. Every subsequent restoreFromTrash — for ANY batch, old or new — hits read_to_string's InvalidData error and fails; every subsequent append's trim_to_cap also fails silently, so the documented 1024-entry cap stops being enforced. The file is never healed; only manually deleting %LOCALAPPDATA%\FileID\trash_log.json recovers the flow (sacrificing all logged batches).
lows.impact: The trash-undo flow (Ctrl+Z after trash, Cleanup restore) is permanently wedged with an opaque "stream did not contain valid UTF-8" failure message; files remain recoverable only by hand from the Recycle Bin, and the sidecar grows unbounded. Contrast: the same module's corrupt-LINE handling (missing HMAC, forged entry, bad JSON) correctly skips per-line — this is the one corruption shape that aborts everything.
lows.suggestedFix: Read bytes (`std::fs::read`) in both read_batch and trim_to_cap, split on b'\n', and per line use `std::str::from_utf8(line)` — on Err, skip (read_batch) or drop (trim_to_cap) that line exactly like the existing HMAC-reject path. The HMAC check already guarantees a lossy/partial line can't be accepted.

lows.title: macOS "Undo last rename" journal has no expiry or file-identity check — a stale persisted batch can silently rename a DIFFERENT file that now occupies newPath and repoint the original file's DB row at it
lows.evidence: undoRenames guards only on path existence: `guard fm.fileExists(atPath: newURL.path) else { skipped += 1; continue }` (ReadStore.swift:986-988) and bails if oldPath is occupied (989-992), then `try fm.moveItem(at: newURL, to: oldURL)` (994) and `UPDATE files SET path_text = ? WHERE id = ?` with the journaled fileID (1004-1007). RenameOutcome stores only fileID/oldPath/newPath (ReadStore.swift:962-964) — no mtime, size, inode, or timestamp. The journal under `bulkRename.lastBatch.v1` persists across launches indefinitely (BulkRenameSheet.swift:158-159 writes it; nothing ages it out), and LibraryView re-surfaces the "Undo last rename" button on every launch while it decodes non-empty (LibraryView.swift:516-519); it is cleared only after a fully clean undo (`if result.failed == 0 && result.skipped == 0`, LibraryView.swift:529-531).
lows.trigger: User bulk-renames photos (IMG_4823.heic → "Mia at the piano.heic") and never undoes; the journal persists for weeks. The user later moves "Mia at the piano.heic" into an album subfolder and a different file lands at the exact journaled newPath (re-export from Photos with the same chosen name, a copy from another device, or restoring an older same-named version) while oldPath stays free. Spotting the still-present "Undo last rename" pill, they click it: the existence-only guards pass, the unrelated file is moved to IMG_4823.heic, and the ORIGINAL file's DB row (journaled fileID, still pointing at newPath since macOS has no rename-heal) gets path_text rewritten to oldPath — now describing the wrong file's bytes with the original's tags/faces/embeddings.
lows.impact: Silent wrong-file rename on disk plus a Library row whose tags/people/CLIP results now describe a different file, reported as success ("Reverted 1 rename"). Narrow trigger, but the journal's unbounded lifetime makes the window unbounded too; the same identity gap applies to the sibling bulkTag.lastBatch.v1 undo (tags stripped from a same-named replacement file). The corrupt-decode and version-bump stories for these journals are otherwise safe (try?-decode → nil).
lows.suggestedFix: Record per-entry identity in RenameOutcome (file size + mtime at rename time, or the URL fileResourceIdentifier) and skip the entry on mismatch, mirroring the Windows engine's file_ref discipline; additionally stamp the journal with a timestamp and stop surfacing the undo affordance after a bounded age (e.g. 7 days) or once the DB row's path no longer equals newPath.

lows.title: Windows engine: non-UTF-16-round-trippable filenames (unpaired surrogates) are silently catalogued under a U+FFFD-mangled path_text — every later action (thumbnail, open, rename, trash, restructure, heal) targets a path that doesn't exist, the incremental-skip never matches, and two such files collapse into one row
lows.line: dbwriter.rs:245 (lossy write), 624 (ON CONFLICT(path_text) collapse); scan_session.rs:217 vs discovery.rs:285-289 (skip-set never matches); consumers: commands/bulk.rs:230, commands/thumbnail.rs:39, commands/restructure.rs:71, commands/trash.rs:53 (PathBuf::from(db text))
lows.evidence: Discovery emits the true `PathBuf` (WTF-8 preserves unpaired surrogates) and the tagging/decode stages open `file.path` directly, so the file scans fine. But dbwriter.rs:245 `let path_text = f.path.to_string_lossy();` replaces each unpaired surrogate with U+FFFD before storing, and that mangled string is the only identity the rest of the system ever sees: bulk.rs:230 / thumbnail.rs:39 / restructure.rs:71 / trash.rs:53 all do `PathBuf::from(<path_text from DB>)`, which on Windows re-encodes U+FFFD literally — a different file name than the on-disk one. The incremental skip set is built from DB text (`set.insert(std::path::PathBuf::from(p))`, scan_session.rs:217) and compared against the real path (`skip_paths.contains(&path)`, discovery.rs:289), so it never matches. `ON CONFLICT(path_text) DO UPDATE` (dbwriter.rs:624) means two distinct files whose names differ only in invalid UTF-16 units lossy-map to the same path_text and upsert into ONE row. Heal can't recover anything: content-hash heal (HEAL_UPDATE_SQL, dbwriter.rs:714) just re-stamps the same lossy text, and stable_path_hash (path_safety.rs:108-114) is computed from the lossy string.
lows.trigger: A file with an unpaired surrogate in its NTFS name (legal on NTFS; produced by buggy archive extractors, old Java/rsync tooling, or SMB hosts) sits in the scanned root. Scan succeeds and the file appears in Library with tags/embedding. User clicks the tile (no thumbnail — File.Exists is false on the U+FFFD path), tries Open/Reveal (SafeOpen returns false), Apply rename ('not found'), trash, or includes it in a restructure (counted failed). Every rescan re-decodes and re-embeds it because the skip set misses.
lows.impact: For the affected (rare) file class: a permanently inert catalog entry — visible and searchable but un-openable, un-renamable, un-trashable, un-movable, with no error explaining why; wasted full reprocessing on every incremental rescan; and in the two-file collision case one file silently disappears from the catalog entirely. No on-disk data is damaged.
lows.suggestedFix: Detect non-round-trippable paths at discovery time (`entry.path().to_str().is_none()` after strip_extended_length — i.e. `to_string_lossy` would be lossy): either skip the file and count it in error_count/discovery_partial with a redacted warn (cheapest, honest), or persist a reversible encoding (percent-encode the raw WTF-8/UTF-16 units into path_text) so DB text round-trips to the exact OsString. At minimum, write the row with failed=1 and an explanatory error_message instead of cataloguing it as healthy.

lows.title: macOS path_hash is Swift's per-process-randomized String.hashValue — violates the stable, case-insensitive path_hash contract the Windows engine documents for the shared schema (latent: column is write-only today)
lows.line: DBWriter.swift:323 (hashValue), 383 (path_hash = excluded.path_hash rewrite); contrast platforms/windows/src/engine/src/util/path_safety.rs:95-114; Database.swift:60 (indexed column)
lows.evidence: DBWriter.swift:323 `let pathHash = Int(bitPattern: UInt(truncatingIfNeeded: file.url.path.hashValue))` — Swift's String.hashValue is seeded with a per-process random value (SipHash), so the same path yields a different path_hash every engine launch, and the upsert rewrites it each rescan (`path_hash = excluded.path_hash`, DBWriter.swift:383), churning the index (Database.swift:60 `.indexed()`). The Windows side defines the column's semantics as stable and case-insensitive (path_safety.rs:95-107: "Case-insensitive stable hash … a re-scan after a path-case change must produce the same hash — else the next ingest creates a duplicate files row … The wire schema stores the resulting i64 as-is") and pins it with proptests (path_safety.rs:320-337). The R0 verifiedClean entry 'ENG-91 path_hash consistency' covered only the Windows writers; no record anywhere covers the macOS writer.
lows.trigger: Any two macOS engine launches: scan, quit, rescan — every files row's path_hash changes despite identical paths. A library DB written on macOS then opened by any current-or-future consumer that joins/dedupes on path_hash (the documented cross-platform i64 contract) sees values that match nothing, not even themselves across sessions.
lows.impact: Latent today (no reader queries path_hash on either platform — verified by grep), so no user-visible behavior yet; but it silently breaks the schema-lockstep claim the column exists for, guarantees wasted index writes every rescan, and is a trap for the first heal/dedupe feature that reads it (the Windows-style path-case heal would mint duplicate rows on macOS).
lows.suggestedFix: Port stable_path_hash semantics to Swift: hash the lowercased UTF-8 path bytes with a fixed-key/deterministic hash (e.g. SipHash-1-3 with constant keys to match Rust's DefaultHasher recipe, or document+share a simpler FNV/truncated-blake3 in the IPC schema) and add a SharedTests pin mirroring path_safety.rs's determinism + case-insensitivity proptests.

lows.title: metadata_synth_failed and deep_loadcontainer_threw log raw "\(error)" whose NSError text embeds the full unredacted path — the exact seeded redacted-path-next-to-raw-error pattern, sibling sites the N8 fix missed
lows.evidence: Line 534-535: `JSONLog.shared.warn(ev: "metadata_synth_failed", path: redactPathForLog(metaURL.path), error: "\(error)")` — the catch wraps `try body.write(to: metaURL, atomically: true, encoding: .utf8)` (line 530); a CocoaError from Data/String.write interpolates as an NSError whose description/userInfo (NSFilePath/NSURL/NSUnderlyingError) carries the FULL metaURL path, defeating the redactPathForLog on the adjacent path: param (metaURL is under ~/Documents/huggingface/..., which the state-root pass-through does NOT cover, so the path: param is correctly redacted — and then the error: field leaks it anyway). Line 179-180: `JSONLog.shared.error(ev: "deep_loadcontainer_threw", error: "\(error)")` — VLMModelFactory.loadContainer file errors (missing/corrupt weight under ~/Documents/huggingface/models/<repo>/) embed the full path the same way. Commit 1e5cab3 (N8) fixed exactly this class at Restructure.swift:311-315 ('NSError text embeds both full paths — log domain+code only') and CleanupView.swift:297, but missed these two siblings.
lows.trigger: metadata_synth_failed: Deep Analyze model-metadata sidecar write fails — disk full, ~/Documents permissions/iCloud-evicted directory, read-only volume. deep_loadcontainer_threw: any VLM load failure (user deleted a weight file, partial download, corrupt safetensors) — a common path on first-run hiccups.
lows.impact: scan.jsonl (the log PRIVACY.md tells users is safe to share: 'Sharing a log file doesn't leak your username + folder layout') gains lines containing /Users/<name>/Documents/huggingface/... — username plus Documents layout.
lows.suggestedFix: Mirror Restructure.swift:312-315: `let ns = error as NSError` and log `"\(ns.domain) \(ns.code)"` (optionally plus redactPathForLog of the known path) instead of "\(error)" at both sites.

lows.title: EngineClient app.log persists raw IPC frame dumps and the raw engine binary path — torn/undecodable frames can write full user file paths (deepAnalyzeProgress.currentPath) to disk
lows.evidence: Line 196: `Self.debug("spawn: starting engine at \(binary.path)")` — raw bundle path. Line 259: `Self.debug("ENGINE: \(String(data: line.prefix(512), encoding: .utf8) ?? "<binary>")")` — any drained line not starting with 0x7B is dumped raw. Line 267: `Self.debug("ENGINE: undecodable frame: \(String(data: line.prefix(512)...)")` — failed decodes dumped raw. Line 275: `Self.debug("spawn: proc.run() FAILED: \(error)")` — NSError embeds the binary path. Self.debug (lines 284-302) appends to ~/Library/Application Support/FileID/logs/app.log persistently. The engine emits deepAnalyzeProgress events whose currentPath is the FULL raw user photo path at up to 4 Hz during caption streaming (DeepAnalyzeRunner.swift:206-225, `currentPath: target.path`).
lows.trigger: (a) Torn frame: if the app stalls and the stderr buffer exceeds maxFrameBytes, the S5 path (lines 247-250) discards the partial buffer and resyncs mid-frame — the next drained chunk doesn't start with '{' and is dumped raw at line 259, potentially containing `"currentPath":"/Users/<name>/Photos/Mom_Birthday_2024/IMG_001.jpg"`. (b) Version skew (updated app + old running engine or vice versa): any event with an unknown payload key fails the synthesized Codable decode and the whole frame — raw paths included — is dumped at line 267. (c) Lines 196/275 leak unconditionally whenever the app runs from a user-owned location (~/Downloads/FileID.app, the run.sh dev tree).
lows.impact: Full user file paths and/or username land unredacted in app.log — the M18 class (raw user paths in app-side log sites) that was found and fixed on Windows (ThumbnailService.cs) but never swept on macOS.
lows.suggestedFix: At 259/267 log only the frame length and the event-kind needle (or scrub with a regex that rewrites "/Users/<segment>/" → "~/") before persisting; wrap binary.path at 196 with redactPathForLog and log only domain+code at 275.

lows.title: redactPathForLog keeps the immediate parent component, so files directly under a home directory redact to "…/<username>/<file>" — the L17 sibling-username class, present in both the Swift and Rust engines, never recorded
lows.evidence: `let parts = (path as NSString).pathComponents; let tail = parts.suffix(2).joined(separator: "/"); return tail.isEmpty ? "…" : "…/\(tail)"` — for /Users/adamnolle/notes.txt the output is "…/adamnolle/notes.txt": the username IS the kept component. The helper's own doc comment (lines 12-14) claims 'folder names like "Mom_Birthday_2024" don't end up in logs', yet suffix(2) always keeps the immediate parent folder name, so /Users/x/Photos/Mom_Birthday_2024/IMG_001.jpg logs as "…/Mom_Birthday_2024/IMG_001.jpg". The Windows APP redactor explicitly guards the home boundary (PathRedactor.cs:30-35, the L17 fix); the engines' last-2-components shape has no equivalent guard and was never audited under this lens (the N8 commit 1e5cab3 fixed only the unanchored Application-Support substring, not the suffix(2) semantics).
lows.trigger: User scans their home folder (a primary use case for a file organizer) — every loose file in ~ that hits a redacted log site (skip_large_file Discovery.swift:107, image_decode_failed Tagging.swift:77, restructure_move_failed Restructure.swift:313-315, crash_recovery_detected FileIDEngineMain.swift:741) writes "…/<username>/<filename>" to scan.jsonl. Scanning a multi-user volume (/Users/bob/...) leaks ANOTHER user's name the same way.
lows.impact: Username (and sensitive parent-folder names) reach scan.jsonl despite redaction, contradicting the helper's comment and PRIVACY.md:34's 'the most directly-identifying segment never reaches the log'.
lows.suggestedFix: Strip a leading home-directory prefix to "~" (with a separator-boundary check) before taking suffix(2) — and if the 2-component tail's first component is a direct child position of /Users/, drop it (emit "…/<file>" only). Port the same guard to platform.rs and pin both with the SharedTests redaction tests added in 1e5cab3.

lows.title: U4's engine-stderr.log is an unredacted persistent sink in the logs directory — third-party library chatter (ONNX Runtime/MLX/Metal C++ stderr) bypasses the path-redaction contract entirely
lows.evidence: bootstrap() opens `~/Library/Application Support/FileID/logs/engine-stderr.log` and `dup2(logFD, STDERR_FILENO)` (lines 41-49) so 'library chatter can never corrupt the event stream' — ALL C/C++ stderr output (ONNX Runtime's default logger, MLX/Metal/ggml diagnostics, anything a dependency prints) is appended verbatim, with rotation but zero redaction. ORT session errors print full model paths (/Users/<name>/Library/Application Support/FileID/Models/... — username), and any dependency that echoes an input path writes it raw. PRIVACY.md:34 promises 'Even local logs redact user file paths before they're written' for files in this exact logs/ directory. This sink was created by this branch's recent U4 fix (Phase 2) and the iterate.sh test asserts the file exists but nothing covers its content.
lows.trigger: Engine start with a CoreML/ORT model load warning or failure (e.g., corrupt clip ONNX → ORT logs the full model path to stderr); any MLX/Metal diagnostic during Deep Analyze. The file accumulates across sessions up to 32 MB before rotating to .1 (still kept).
lows.impact: A user who shares the logs folder per PRIVACY.md's 'attach the file to a GitHub issue' guidance ships an unredacted file: username-bearing paths guaranteed, full user-content paths possible depending on library failure modes — contradicting the document that calls any contradiction 'a release blocker'.
lows.suggestedFix: Either (a) disclose engine-stderr.log as an unredacted diagnostics file in PRIVACY.md + the Settings logs UI, or (b) interpose a line-buffered scrubber (read end of a pipe → rewrite /Users/<seg>/ → ~/ → append) instead of dup2-ing the raw file, or (c) scrub on rotation/engine exit.

lows.title: PRIVACY.md:34 misdescribes all three redaction implementations — neither engine strips the home prefix, and the state-root verbatim pass-through writes /Users/<name>/ into scan.jsonl on every engine start
lows.evidence: PRIVACY.md:34: 'Each strips the home-directory prefix (`C:\\Users\\<name>\\...` or `/Users/<name>/...`) down to `~`, so the most directly-identifying segment never reaches the log. Sharing a log file doesn't leak your username + folder layout.' Only the Windows app's PathRedactor.cs does that. macOS redactPathForLog passes its state root verbatim — `if path.hasPrefix(fileIDStateRoot + "/") { return path }` (PathRedaction.swift:22) where fileIDStateRoot = /Users/<name>/Library/Application Support/FileID — so `JSONLog.shared.info(ev: "clip_model_loaded", path: safe)` (MobileCLIPService.swift:82, safe = redactPathForLog(modelURL.path)) and arcface_model_loaded's extra `"path": AnyCodable(redactPathForLog(url.path))` (ArcFaceService.swift:122) write the full username-bearing path on every model load. The Rust engine does the same for %LOCALAPPDATA%\FileID (platform.rs:23-38). DECISIONS.md:1691 records the last-2/state-root semantics as design, but PRIVACY.md — the user-facing verifiable contract — was never reconciled.
lows.trigger: Every engine start with CLIP or SFace installed logs clip_model_loaded / arcface_model_loaded with the verbatim state-root path; user shares scan.jsonl per PRIVACY.md guidance.
lows.impact: The flagship verifiability promise ('Sharing a log file doesn't leak your username') is false on both engine platforms; PRIVACY.md declares any contradiction a release blocker.
lows.suggestedFix: Cheapest correct fix: in both engines' helpers, rewrite the home-prefix of state-root pass-through paths to '~' (keep the rest verbatim — still fully debuggable: '~/Library/Application Support/FileID/Models/clip.onnx'); then PRIVACY.md:34 becomes accurate for the state-root case. Alternatively amend PRIVACY.md to describe the real last-2-components semantics.

verifiedClean: N1-mac-toctou: ReadStore.applyProposedName (ReadStore.swift:1043-1080) — the fileExists collision-probe loop is TOCTOU-shaped, but FileManager.moveItem atomically refuses an existing destination, so a race yields a reported failure, never a clobber; the M5/L5 busy-timeout + disk rollback are intact (R0 already pins this); path traversal via vlm_proposed_name is blocked at write time because both engines slug-sanitize to [a-z0-9-_] (DeepAnalyze.swift:450-484, byte-faithful mirror of Windows sanitize_proposed_name), so no '/' or '..' can reach appendingPathComponent.

N1-mac-toctou: ReadStore.undoRenames (ReadStore.swift:979-1021) — both-end fileExists guards plus EEXIST-refusing moveItem plus the DB-disagree rollback (1011-1014): no clobber path exists; the worst race outcome is a failed-vs-skipped miscount. RenameOutcome persistence in UserDefaults is same-user-writable but confers no capability a same-user process doesn't already have.

N1-mac-toctou: CleanupView trash flow (CleanupView.swift:256-338) — trashItem moves to the recoverable system Trash (the UI promises exactly that), throws rather than overwrites, operates on the catalogued URL without dereferencing symlinks for the move, and DB rows are pruned only for successful trashes (307-312); no root-escape is possible since the destination is the Trash.

N1-mac-toctou: Plan-time name handling in the app planner (RestructureView.swift:1200-1225) — sanitizePathSegment/sanitizeFilename (1465-1483) strip '/', '.', '..', NUL and leading dots per component, and the lexical root-prefix guard drops anything else, so crafted VLM names and person names cannot traverse at plan time; the remaining gap is apply-time symlink resolution, reported separately as a finding.

N1-mac-toctou: BulkRenameSheet (BulkRenameSheet.swift) — display-and-confirm only; there is no name editor, so users/UI cannot inject arbitrary strings into vlm_proposed_name (the only writers are the two engines' sanitizers via DeepAnalyzeRunner.swift:286-292).

N1-mac-toctou: DeepAnalyzeRunner (engine) — performs zero disk file operations (no moveItem/removeItem/createDirectory on user files); renames are applied app-side via ReadStore; it writes only the parse()-sanitized proposedName to the DB.

N1-mac-toctou: Discovery (Discovery.swift:80-104) — FileManager deep enumeration does not descend into symlinked directories and entries must satisfy isRegularFile, so the catalog cannot ingest out-of-root paths through links that later destructive ops (trash/rename/restructure) would chase.

N1-mac-toctou: Orphan sweep (FileIDEngineMain.swift:570-669) — prefix-scoped half-open range, 5000-row cap, stat-before-delete, person reconcile in-transaction (matches R0's verifiedClean entry); no NEW symlink hazard found: a row whose path routes through a symlink keeps 'existing' while the link exists, and a symlink-file row is purged only when the link itself is gone. (Its interaction with a dropped restructure DB update is the impact chain of the updatePathTexts finding, not a sweep defect.)

N1-mac-toctou: CleanupView keeper auto-tag (TagWriter.addTagsBulk on keeper URLs, CleanupView.swift:321-328) — additive xattr write on files the user chose to keep; not destructive, no path construction from untrusted strings.

N1-mac-toctou: RestructureEngine.apply conflict catches (RestructureView.swift:1397-1409) — the EEXIST-as-atomic-test pattern (comment 1384-1387) is correct for the final path component on both symlink and realMove modes; rename(2)/symlink(2) do not follow a symlink at the destination leaf, so a planted leaf symlink cannot redirect the write (the ancestor-directory case is the separate finding).

N1-mac-toctou: Engine model-dir file ops (DeepAnalyze.swift:215, 499-525 self-heal; VLMDownloader/StreamingDownload) — operate only on app-owned model directories under Application Support/huggingface, not on user library files; outside this lens's destructive-user-file scope and previously audited for download integrity.

N2-resource-exhaustion: macOS image scan decode (Tagging.swift loadCGImage:273-297): NOT the Windows pre-read bomb class — no file bytes are read into memory (CGImageSourceCreateWithURL is file-backed), output is bounded by kCGImageSourceThumbnailMaxPixelSize=512, files >500 MB and <256 B are skipped in Discovery (Discovery.swift:72,106, with the OOM rationale in its header), and concurrency is bounded by VisionWorkerPool(Hardware.workerCap<=32). There is no explicit pixel-count cap like Windows' MAX_DECODED_PIXELS, but containment rides on ImageIO's documented memory-efficient thumbnail downsampling — no in-repo full-resolution allocation exists, so no demonstrable bug (a cheap kCGImagePropertyPixelWidth/Height pre-check would still be sound belt-and-suspenders).

N2-resource-exhaustion: macOS DeepAnalyze video keyframe path: AVAssetImageGenerator.maximumSize = maxPixelSize caps the decoded frame (DeepAnalyze.swift:670); the QuickLook fallback is pixel-bounded with an 8 s hard timeout (611-634); DeepAnalyze's PDF render IS pixel-bounded via scale = maxPixelSize/longSide (694-698) — only the scan-path Tagging.processPDF lacks the bound (reported).

N2-resource-exhaustion: macOS M6 streaming hashes intact: gitBlobHash (DeepAnalyze.swift:550-571) and sha256HexOfFile (StreamingDownload.swift:360-370) stream 4 MB autoreleasepool chunks with defer-closed FileHandles — multi-GB model files never load whole into RAM.

N2-resource-exhaustion: fd lifetimes on 50K-file macOS scans: Discovery walks via FileManager.enumerator (no per-file descriptors retained); Tagging's CGImageSource objects are CF-scoped inside the per-file autoreleasepool (Tagging.swift:70); the only long-lived handles are JSONLog and the IPC wire. No FileHandle leak on the scan path.

N2-resource-exhaustion: WAL growth during giant scans, both platforms: PRAGMA wal_autocheckpoint = 10000 (~40 MB) is set on macOS (Database.swift:34) and Windows (db/mod.rs:24), Windows additionally runs wal_checkpoint(TRUNCATE) at shutdown (main.rs:410-419), and app readers are short-lived fresh connections so checkpoints aren't starved — WAL stays bounded.

N2-resource-exhaustion: Windows ThumbnailDiskCache under disk-full: writes are temp+rename with the .tmp deleted on failure (ThumbnailDiskCache.cs:178-190), every IO failure is caught and warn-logged (206-209), and the 500 MB cap has a 1.2x hard-ceiling immediate sweep (34-40, 213-227) — disk-full degrades to log noise; no corruption or unbounded growth. (The 06-03 sweep-overshoot low and hard-ceiling fix are prior records; nothing new.)

N2-resource-exhaustion: Windows downloader disk-full mid-range-write mechanics: write_all errors propagate immediately via `?` (downloader.rs:969-970) — no retry-budget burn, no spin; .part files are retained deliberately for resume; SHA mismatch purges combined + all parts (749-766); the R0 stall-budget finding has been fixed by the progress-aware RetryBudget (848-887). Only the user-facing ENOSPC classification is weak (reported separately as low).

N2-resource-exhaustion: Windows RAM++/CLIP batch coordinators under MemoryTier::Low: pending batch inputs are bounded by worker count (each worker awaits its oneshot before submitting another frame); Low tier clamps workers to 6 (scan.rs:272-273) and the CLIP session pool to 1 (tagging.rs:386-389) — coordinator memory is bounded. (The frame-vs-byte predecode budget is the separate reported finding.)

N2-resource-exhaustion: macOS DBWriter transient retry policy: SQLITE_FULL is correctly excluded from the transient set (isTransient = BUSY/LOCKED/IOERR only, DBWriter.swift:303-306) and the retry loop caps at 3 attempts with backoff — no ENOSPC spin. (The scan-continues-after-failure behavior is the separate reported low.)

N2-resource-exhaustion: macOS StreamingDownload disk-full mid-concat/promote: a writer.write throw removes finalTemp and all parts before rethrowing (StreamingDownload.swift:248-253); assembled-size and SHA checks delete on mismatch (256-271); the single-stream path deletes its temp on checksum/move failure — no corrupt model is ever promoted on a full disk.

N2-resource-exhaustion: R0 fixes spot-checked while tracing this lens are correctly landed at HEAD: the rescan force flag is now threaded into DBWriter (DBWriter.swift:117-123, FileIDEngineMain.swift:490-494), failed files no longer clobber child rows (DBWriter.swift:338-344), and the seven ThumbnailService.cs raw-path log sites are now PathRedactor-wrapped (246, 251, 287).

N3-error-paths: macOS DBWriter.commit error path: buffer is detached before the write, the retry re-runs idempotent upserts from a value copy, and on hard failure counters/resume-cursor stay consistently unbumped with a categorized db_write_failed event — no partial transaction state (whole batch rolls back atomically; only the insertOne doc comment overclaims per-file rollback).

N3-error-paths: macOS DBWriter post-R0 fixes verified in current tree: forceReprocess is threaded (init 117-124, gate 350), failed-file early-return preserves child rows (338-344), and the unchanged-path CLIP backfill exists (433-442) — R0 C1/C2 correctly landed.

N3-error-paths: macOS JobQueue drainer: the pop-nil -> withCheckedContinuation re-check (98-104) closes the enqueue/park race under actor reentrancy; `running` is cleared after every job (run closures are non-throwing by type); cancelPending leaves the waiter consistent.

N3-error-paths: macOS StreamingDownload.parallelStreamingDownload: every failure exit (range error, rangeNotSupported fallback, concat I/O, size mismatch, checksum mismatch, promote failure) removes part files and finalTemp; the double-resume guard (completion=nil before cancel-at-submit) holds on both single and range paths.

N3-error-paths: macOS CLIPModelInstaller.runHubFetch: staging dir is removed by defer on all exits, every return sets a terminal status, cancellation maps to .missing("Cancelled."); the promote loop's non-atomicity across 4 files is self-healing on retry (identical staged content, refreshStatus reports missing).

N3-error-paths: macOS VLMDownloader sentinel discipline: .fileid-verified-<revision> is written only after the full loop (or all-skipped verification) completes; any failure/cancel exit leaves no sentinel so the next fetch re-proves LFS sha256 before trusting on-disk files; hostile-path continues are unreachable for manifest-pinned repos.

N3-error-paths: macOS DeepAnalyzeRunner: processed/failed counters are persist-gated (L8 intact); deep_model_too_big, deep_load_failed, empty-target, and cancel paths all emit deepAnalyzeComplete; deep_targets_failed omits the complete event but the app-side M10 deep-prefix clear covers the flag, so no user-visible wedge.

N3-error-paths: Windows commands/scan.rs writer-error arm: scan_state is released and PhaseChanged(Failed) + scan_failed are emitted on Err (290-305) — the app is not left believing a scan is active (the residual scan_sessions stamp gap is reported separately).

N3-error-paths: Windows prewarm.rs in-flight dedupe and download-failure arms: ReleaseOnDrop clears IN_FLIGHT on every exit including panics; all download/zip failure exits emit model_kind-stamped events the app routes (model_download_failed, download_tls_pin_failed, zip_extract_failed, prewarm_cancelled), and the new pin-failure classification (download_failure_kind_and_hint + chain_has_pin_failure) feeds an app-routed kind on both the parallel and zip paths.

N3-error-paths: Windows ModelInstallerService.Reset(): all five slots (incl. RamPlus/DeepVlm/Accelerator) FailIfDownloading on engine respawn, and PrewarmAsync sets a terminal Failed on every WaitForReadyAsync throw path before any IPC send — no slot is left Downloading by those throw paths.

N3-error-paths: Windows prewarm per-kind cancel registry mechanics (prewarm_cancel_flag / reset_prewarm_cancel / cancel_prewarm): create-or-set semantics close the lazy-create race and per-kind isolation is test-pinned; the remaining cold-start lost-cancel is app-side and reported as the incomplete-C7 finding.

N3-error-paths: macOS scan-cancel paths already on record: ScanCoordinator queued-cancel drop and markSessionFinal-after-cancel are R0 preexisting items #16/#17 — confirmed still accurate at HEAD, not re-reported.

N5-migration-compat: Windows crash-mid-migration (lens b): each migration runs in its own transaction — migrations.rs:108-115 wraps execute_batch(sql) AND the grdb_migrations identifier INSERT in one unchecked_transaction before commit; a kill mid-migration rolls back both, restart re-applies cleanly.

N5-migration-compat: macOS crash-mid-migration (lens b): GRDB Migration.run wraps the migration body + updateAppliedIdentifier in a single db.inTransaction(.immediate) (GRDB checkout Migration.swift:56-61), so no migration can be half-applied or applied-but-unrecorded.

N5-migration-compat: Interrupted first-run, kill during v1 (lens e): clean restart on both stacks — per-migration transactions roll back; the grdb_migrations DDL is CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS on both (DatabaseMigrator.swift:615, migrations.rs:19-23,90) and all v1 DDL is idempotent (IF NOT EXISTS) anyway on Windows.

N5-migration-compat: macOS v14 'rebuild' on a desynced/stale FTS index (lens c): FTS5 rebuild deletes the entire index then re-scans the content table (never parses the corrupt structures); R0 already empirically verified rebuild resyncs a populated external-content index — and even if Database() init threw, FileIDEngineMain.swift:54-64 degrades to a db_open_failed event with database=nil rather than crash-looping/bricking startup.

N5-migration-compat: Dev-DB that applied the pre-renumber 'v12_fts_sync_triggers' identifier (merge-record hazard): confirmed benign exactly as R0 recorded — GRDB filters applied ids to known ones (DatabaseMigrator.swift:618), no fatalError; the registered v14_fts_sync_triggers then re-runs idempotently (CREATE TRIGGER IF NOT EXISTS + harmless re-rebuild). Dedupe: already in R0 verifiedClean, re-verified against GRDB 7.x source.

N5-migration-compat: Column-for-column schema parity v1→v13 (lens d): every table/column/affinity/constraint in migrations.rs V1-V13 matches Database.swift v1-v13 including UNIQUE ON CONFLICT REPLACE on files.path_text, identical FTS5 tokenizers ('porter unicode61'), identical v12 child→parent wipe order, and identical v13 face_a/face_b columns; pinned by the Rust fresh_database_applies_all_migrations test (23 files cols / 11 persons cols / v13 columns). The fork is confined to slot v14 (reported above) — schema objects (triggers/index), not columns.
